staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen protocol, allergy menu, or staff allergy-handling procedure has been documented in any public source reviewed. The restaurant does note it will try to accommodate guest requests, but this is framed around taste preferences, not food safety.

positives.text: Chef adapts dishes to guest preferences on request — guests with dietary needs who call ahead may be able to arrange a custom plate, as the menu is inherently flexible and not bound to a printed list.

caveats: Cash only at lunch; cards accepted at dinner. Preschool children are not permitted. Lunch reservations by phone only (052-532-6262); dinner reservations available online. Private groups of 10–30 can book the full space. Parking: 2 spaces behind the building plus a paid coin lot adjacent. No GF or vegan credentials found in any source; celiac and strict vegan diners should call ahead.

name_ja: フジヤマ55 金シャチ横丁店
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市中区二の丸1 金シャチ横丁 宗春ゾーン
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch on 愛知県名古屋市中区二の丸１番; coordinates fall within the Kinshachi Yokocho complex, ~275m south of Nagoya Castle main keep.
neighborhood: 名古屋城, 金シャチ横丁宗春ゾーン, 市役所駅
cuisine: ラーメン / つけめん
vegan_detail: All broths are built on pork bone (tonkotsu) and whole chicken stock, combined with bonito, horse mackerel, and mackerel dashi. No plant-based broth or vegan menu items are offered. The Taiwan mazesoba also uses meat-based seasoning.
gf_detail: Noodles are house-made daily from Aichi-brand wheat flour (きぬあかり/ゆめあかり) plus kansui and potato starch — wheat is the primary ingredient. Broths contain fish-based dashi (bonito, mackerel) which typically include soy sauce with wheat. No gluten-free noodle substitute or GF accommodation is listed anywhere. Celiac diners should avoid.
hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 (LO 14:30), 17:00–22:00 (LO 21:00); hours may shorten on certain dates — verify with venue
seats_text: 19 seats total: 11 counter, 8 table
cultural_comfort_note: Counter-heavy ramen shop inside a tourist-oriented food hall next to Nagoya Castle; staff interaction is minimal and ordering is straightforward, but menus and signage are primarily in Japanese.
caveats: Hours vary and the venue lists irregular closure days — confirm before visiting. Payment info conflicts across sources (Tabelog EN lists cards accepted; HotPepper implies cash); cash on hand recommended. The Kinshachi Yokocho complex itself may have separate opening/closing times that affect access.

bio.background: Ryuichiro Sawa was born in Nagoya in 1971. After five years in the corporate sector, he pursued his passion for ramen by opening Chuuka Soba Tsurumaiikkokkuya, which drew lines from its very first month. While researching ramen across Japan, he encountered tsukemen — then a Tokyo phenomenon — and set out to bring it to Nagoya. He founded Fujiyama55 in the Osu district in 2009, widely credited with igniting Nagoya's concentrated-tsukemen boom and dispelling the city's reputation as a ramen backwater.
bio.origin: 名古屋市大須 (Nagoya, Osu district), 2009
bio.specialty: 魚介濃厚つけ麺 — thick house-made wheat noodles served alongside a two-day-simmered tonkotsu-torigara broth enriched with bonito, horse mackerel, and mackerel dashi; the Kinshachi Yokocho branch also features Nagoya miso ramen and Taiwan mazesoba
bio.philosophy: Sawa's guiding idea: 'I want to do fun things with my team — work itself is the highest form of entertainment.' The brand's three pillars are people development, flavor creation, and store excellence. Noodles are hand-crafted each morning using only Aichi-brand wheat (きぬあかり/ゆめあかり), kansui, and potato starch — no preservatives or additives — with the ratio adjusted daily for temperature and humidity.

anecdotes.text: Sawa consumes over 500 bowls of ramen per year as part of his ongoing research into flavor.

The Fujiyama55 name reflects a twin ambition: Mount Fuji-inspired generous portions and the 'go-go' energy of relentless expansion — the chain now operates 59+ locations across Japan, Taiwan, China, Hong Kong, Thailand, Indonesia, and France.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire kitchen is built around wheat-based noodles made fresh on-site daily; cross-contamination with gluten is certain and unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broths combine pork bone, chicken, and fish dashi; standard Japanese soy sauce (containing wheat) is almost certainly used in seasoning tare — no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every broth on the menu contains pork bone, chicken stock, and/or fish dashi (bonito, mackerel, horse mackerel). There is no plant-based alternative and shared cooking equipment makes vegan cross-contact unavoidable.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, allergy statement, or staff allergy protocol is published by the restaurant or venue. Diners with serious allergies should call ahead: 052-212-7375.

positives.text: Noodles are made with only three ingredients (wheat flour, kansui, potato starch) and zero preservatives or additives, which simplifies the ingredient picture even if it does not remove wheat.

name_ja: 贅食家千壽 丸の内店
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市中区丸の内3-1-34
geocode_note: GSI geocode returned lat 35.176945, lng 136.901443 for 丸の内三丁目１番３４号. Approximately 0.98 km from Nagoya Castle — within the 1.61 km target radius.
neighborhood: 中区丸の内（丸の内駅徒歩約6分）
seats_text: 16席、カウンター席あり、全席禁煙
hours_raw: ランチ 11:00-15:00、ディナー 17:00-20:00（毎日）。定休日不明（不定休の可能性あり）。
closed_days: 定休日の記載なし（不定休の可能性あり）
cultural_comfort_note: The restaurant is a casual sushi counter with no English-language presence found; basic Japanese will go a long way, though counter sushi shops are generally navigable by pointing at items.
vegan_detail: This is a traditional sushi and seafood izakaya. Miso soup uses seafood-based dashi (海鮮出汁). Fish, shellfish, and animal-derived broths are core to the menu. No plant-based or vegan alternatives were found.
gf_detail: Standard wheat-containing soy sauce (醤油) is used for sushi and sashimi — the default in Japanese sushi restaurants. Dashi is seafood-based. No gluten-free soy sauce, tamari, or dedicated GF preparations were found or advertised. Tempura appears on the parent location's menu, indicating shared-kitchen wheat use. Cross-contamination risk is real. Diners with celiac disease should contact the restaurant directly before visiting; do not assume safety.

bio.chef_name: 亀田俊之 (Kameda Toshiyuki) — main location
bio.background: 贅食家千壽は名古屋市北区黒川に本店を構える寿司居酒屋で、丸の内店は2026年4月5日に新規オープンした支店。本店では店主の亀田俊之氏が北部市場で丸ごと一本の魚を自ら仕入れ、試食して品質を確認するスタイルを貫いている。
bio.specialty: にぎり寿司（1貫160円からの圧倒的コスパ）、あたりまえランチ（¥1,990で10貫のにぎりとA5飛騨牛）
bio.philosophy: 「贅沢をお値打ちに」——高品質な寿司を手の届く価格で提供することを使命とし、値上げをせずに赤字覚悟で運営を続ける姿勢が話題を呼んでいる。
bio.origin: 名古屋市北区（本店）、丸の内店は2026年4月開業

anecdotes.text: 本店はテレビ愛知で特集放送後に売上が約2倍に。店主は設備増強のために最大限の融資を受けたにもかかわらず、価格を据え置き続けている。

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on dedicated fryers or separation of wheat-containing preparations. The parent menu includes tempura, indicating wheat flour is used in the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is the default at sushi restaurants. No gluten-free soy sauce or tamari was mentioned anywhere in available sources.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Seafood-based dashi (海鮮出汁) is used in miso soup. Fish and shellfish are prepared throughout the kitchen. No plant-based separation is available.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, allergen labeling, or staff allergy-handling protocol was found in any source. Celiac diners should call ahead: 052-201-3655.

positives.text: Counter-style seating means you can communicate directly with the chef about what goes into each piece, which may allow some customization.

caveats: This is a very new restaurant (opened April 5, 2026) with limited review data (8 reviews on Tabelog as of research date). Hours and closed days may not yet be fully settled — verify before visiting. Chef attribution (Kameda Toshiyuki) comes from the main Kurokawa location; the Marunouchi branch chef is not independently confirmed. No allergen or GF information exists in any source; celiac diners must call before visiting.

name_ja: あんかけスパゲティ NANAYA
cuisine: あんかけスパゲティ（名古屋めし）
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市中区丸の内2-14-21 サンマンション丸の内 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch on 愛知県名古屋市中区丸の内二丁目１４番２１号; coordinates confirmed within target box and ~1.26km from Nagoya Castle.
neighborhood: 丸の内／名古屋市中区
seats_text: 18席（カウンター席あり）
cultural_comfort_note: Small 18-seat counter-focused shop with no English menu noted; ordering is manageable by pointing at the menu, but staff are unlikely to speak English.
vegan_detail: Ankake spaghetti is a wheat-pasta dish fried in lard with a thick tomato-pepper sauce; standard toppings include wieners, bacon, and egg. No plant-based or vegan options are indicated anywhere on the menu or in reviews.
gf_detail: The core dish is wheat spaghetti — gluten is structural, not incidental. The sauce may also contain soy sauce (wheat-bearing) as a seasoning. No gluten-free pasta or allergen-accommodation is mentioned in any source. This restaurant is not suitable for celiac diners.

bio.background: NANAYA opened on 24 October 2022 in a ground-floor space at Sun Mansion Marunouchi, a short walk from Marunouchi Station. Reviews note it is a compact, counter-led shop described as run by a kind couple, though no further owner details are publicly documented. A Tabelog review mentions the shop relocated from a previous basement location, suggesting the owners had an earlier incarnation before the current address.
bio.specialty: Ankake spaghetti — the classic Nagoya-born dish of thick wheat noodles stir-fried in lard and blanketed in a spicy, starch-thickened tomato-pepper sauce. NANAYA's version is praised for its punchy, peppery sauce and satisfying chew. Popular variations include the Mirakan (topped with a fried egg) and seasonal specials such as a Menchi Katsu (breaded minced-meat cutlet) topping in spring.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers on Tabelog consistently highlight the vibrant, cheerful atmosphere of the small counter space, and note the shop relocated from a basement location before settling at the current Sun Mansion Marunouchi address in late 2022.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The restaurant's core product is wheat spaghetti; the kitchen works exclusively with wheat pasta and there is no indication of any gluten-free preparation area or protocols.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Ankake spaghetti sauce may contain soy sauce, which in Japan standardly contains wheat. No source confirms NANAYA uses tamari or any gluten-free soy sauce alternative.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Noodles are fried in lard and standard toppings include wieners and egg; no plant-only preparation line exists.

caveats: Hours from Tabelog (Mon-Fri dinner L.O. 20:00, Sat lunch only L.O. 14:00); TravelJP lists dinner until 22:00 — the Tabelog listing is treated as authoritative. Cash and PayPay only; no credit cards. No official website exists; Instagram is the only owned online presence. Owner/chef identity not publicly documented. The 'holidays closed' note on Tabelog means national holidays should be treated as closed unless confirmed otherwise.

name_ja: 創作串揚げ つだ 金シャチ横丁店
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市中区二の丸1番2・3 金シャチ横丁 東門エリア 宗春ゾーン
geocode_note: GSI address search on 愛知県名古屋市中区二の丸１番 returned [136.90155, 35.183643]; 275m from Nagoya Castle target point — within 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 中区二の丸（金シャチ横丁・宗春ゾーン）
seats_text: 30席（カウンター18席、テーブル12席）
cultural_comfort_note: Counter-style omakase dining with course service; English is not widely spoken, but online reservations are available and the format is intuitive for non-Japanese speakers.
vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu. Courses are heavily meat- and seafood-focused (wagyu, live prawn, hamo, scallop, salmon roe). Vegetable skewers do appear in courses (asparagus, green pea croquette, ginkgo nuts), but they share oil with meat/seafood, the batter very likely contains eggs, and dipping sauces include a blended soy sauce and a house sauce whose exact ingredients are unlisted. Hidden dashi (katsuo/bonito) is possible in sauces. Vegan guests should call ahead and ask explicitly — ad-hoc accommodation is noted as possible (フードアレルギー対応可) but a fully vegan course is not offered.
gf_detail: Kushiage is deep-fried in wheat-flour batter (パン粉 breadcrumbs over a flour-egg-based coat) — standard wheat throughout. Frying oil is a lard-and-vegetable-oil blend used for all items; there is no dedicated GF fryer. Dipping options include a blended soy sauce (合わせ醤油) that almost certainly contains wheat, plus salt, lemon, and sansho salt which are likely safe. The restaurant states food-allergy accommodation is available (フードアレルギー対応可), so it is worth calling ahead, but celiac-safe dining cannot be assumed without direct confirmation.

bio.background: Tsuda Takeshi graduated from an Osaka culinary school and spent five years mastering French cuisine before a single meal at Rokakutei — a Michelin one-star kushikatsu specialist with locations in Osaka and Tokyo — changed his direction entirely. He joined Rokakutei and spent a decade there as its lead fryer, absorbing the techniques that would earn the restaurant its star. In March 2013, after 25 years in the craft, he opened his own flagship in Nagoya's Higashi Ward. The Kinshachi Yokocho branch opened on 29 March 2018, the same month Nagoya Castle's gourmet street debuted, and a Tokyo outpost at Toranomon Hills followed in 2024.
bio.specialty: Seasonal omakase kushiage course — live tiger prawn, Omi beef Tomosankaku, Aomori scallop, hamo with cream cheese, and salmon-with-ikura ('parent-and-child skewer') are signature items
bio.philosophy: Tsuda's guiding principle is that the ingredient is the star, not the batter. His coating is deliberately as thin as possible — built up with meringue and milk for lightness, finished with very fine breadcrumbs — and fried slowly at 170°C to coax natural flavour forward. He treats kushiage as washoku, using only sushi-grade ingredients that could in principle be eaten raw, and serves each skewer at the moment it is perfect.

anecdotes.text: Tsuda has said he was shocked when he first ate at Rokakutei: he had assumed kushiage was simply 'put batter on, fry, dip in sauce' — the experience upended that assumption and sent him on a 25-year journey to elevate the form.
A secret menu item — fried-rice-cracker tea rice — became so popular with regulars it was eventually added to the standard course.
Tsuda has publicly stated his next ambitions: Tokyo recognition by 2026 and a potential New York outpost by age 64, positioning creative kushiage as a global fine-dining form.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All kushiage items share the same lard-and-vegetable-oil frying blend; there is no separate dedicated GF fryer. Wheat from standard batter will be present in the oil throughout service.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house dipping options include a blended soy sauce (合わせ醤油, described as 30+ ingredient base) which in standard Japanese practice contains wheat. Salt, sansho salt, lemon, and mustard options exist and are likely wheat-free, but confirmation is required.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All skewers are fried in the same shared oil (lard-and-vegetable blend). Lard is animal-derived. Even vegetable skewers are cooked in lard-containing oil alongside meat and seafood items.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Hitosara lists フードアレルギー対応可 (food-allergy accommodation available) for this location. The nature and extent of accommodation is not further specified; direct inquiry before visiting is strongly recommended.

positives.text: The restaurant uses exclusively domestic, sushi-grade ingredients and communicates openly about provenance — the counter format allows direct conversation with the chef about what is in each skewer.

caveats: Hours have seasonal irregularities (Wednesdays open in April; additional ad-hoc closures noted). The allergy-accommodation claim (フードアレルギー対応可) comes from Hitosara and has not been independently verified via direct contact. Celiac or vegan diners should call the restaurant before visiting. Lard in the frying oil is a hard blocker for vegan guests and a cross-contamination concern for all. Soy-sauce wheat status should be confirmed directly.

address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市西区上名古屋3-25-14
geocode_note: GSI AddressSearch returned [136.890778, 35.195278] for 愛知県名古屋市西区上名古屋三丁目25番14号. Approximately 1,349m from Nagoya Castle (35.1856, 136.8997) — within the 1.61km target radius.
neighborhood: Kaminagoya, Nishi Ward — 4-minute walk north of Joshin Station (Tsurumai Line Exit 1)
seats_text: Roughly 12 seats across counter and table spots
cultural_comfort_note: QR-code ordering system for meals; Japanese-only menu and signage, but the ordering flow is straightforward and staff interaction is minimal.
gf_detail: Standard machi-chuka kitchen — soy sauce (wheat-containing) is a baseline ingredient across virtually every dish including the signature ketchup-rice omurice. No gluten-free options, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodation mentioned on Tabelog, the official Instagram, or any reviewed source. Wheat exposure is near-certain; not safe for celiac or serious gluten intolerance.
vegan_detail: Signature omurice is built on eggs and likely pork-based fried rice; other menu items include pork, meat-based broths, and lard-fried dishes typical of machi-chuka style. No plant-based options identified.

bio.background: Noma-san spent his working life as an electrical equipment salesman before pivoting to professional cooking at age 40 — a late-start story that became a local talking point. He trained at several Chinese restaurants, including running Taka-hanten in the Endonji shotengai, before opening Nagoya Hanten in December 2025.
bio.specialty: 町中華本気のオムライス (machi-chuka honki no omurice) — ketchup fried rice sautéed in a wok, blanketed in a soft, barely-set sheet of premium Miyazaki 康卵 eggs at roughly ¥100 per egg, five per plate.
bio.philosophy: Noma's stated goal was to make an omurice that could only come from a Chinese restaurant — combining the technique of wok-fried rice with the silky egg work of tenshin-don. He refuses to compromise on ingredients despite a cost ratio above 50%.

anecdotes.text: Despite each premium Miyazaki egg costing around ¥100, Noma uses over 200 eggs a day and doesn't blink at the food-cost math — 'without the right egg, it just doesn't taste right,' he has said.
The rice is Akita Komachi from Akita Prefecture, chosen for its firmness and mild sweetness when stir-fried — Noma considers it non-negotiable for both the fried rice and the omurice base.
The shop drew daily queues almost from its first week of trading, less than two months after opening in December 2025.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on cross-contamination controls available; standard machi-chuka kitchen implies shared woks and utensils used across all dishes.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Wheat-containing soy sauce is a foundational ingredient in Chinese restaurant cooking and almost certainly used across sauces, marinades, and stir-fry bases here; no tamari or GF soy sauce alternative mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Shared wok cooking with meat, pork mince (Taiwan dishes), and animal-based stocks makes vegan cross-contact unavoidable.

caveats: Hours from Tabelog (lunch + dinner Tue-Sun); early blog posts from Dec 2025 described dinner-only service, suggesting lunch was added after opening. Confirm current hours before visiting. Seat count (~12) is from a Dec 2025 blog report and may have changed. No phone number publicly listed; reservation by phone is reportedly sometimes possible per Tabelog note.

name_ja: ひつまぶし名古屋備長 金シャチ横丁店
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市中区三の丸1丁目2番5号 金シャチ横丁義直ゾーン内
geocode_note: GSI address search on 三の丸一丁目２番 returned [136.89917, 35.182224]; approximately 378m from Nagoya Castle center (35.1856, 136.8997), well within 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 中区三の丸 (金シャチ横丁)
hours_raw: 平日 10:30–17:30（LO 16:00）、土日祝 10:30–15:30（LO 15:00）・16:30–19:00（LO 18:30）。月曜定休（月曜祝日の場合は翌日）。名古屋城の休城日に準ずる。うなぎ売り切れ次第終了。
closed_days: 月曜日（祝日の場合は翌日）、名古屋城休城日
seats_text: 36 table seats (9 tables) + 4 counter seats
cultural_comfort_note: Located in the tourist-oriented Kinshachi Yokocho district next to Nagoya Castle; staff are accustomed to international visitors but the menu and ordering experience remain primarily Japanese-language.
vegan_detail: Eel is the sole focus of the menu. The signature ochazuke eating method uses fish-based dashi broth. No plant-based alternatives exist. Not suitable for vegans.
gf_detail: The house tare (sauce) is made with tamari soy sauce, mirin, and sugar. Traditional tamari contains little or no wheat compared to standard koikuchi soy sauce, but Bincho has not publicly confirmed their tamari is wheat-free or certified gluten-free. The eel itself is grilled over binchotan charcoal with no breading. The ochazuke broth (dashi) does not contain wheat but is fish-based. No dedicated GF menu or official allergy accommodation policy is published. Celiac diners should contact the restaurant directly before visiting.

bio.background: Bincho Co., Ltd. was founded in September 1992 by Hiroshi Suzuki, a native of Aichi Prefecture. After completing a decade-long apprenticeship at Unagi no Nishimoto, a longstanding Nagoya eel establishment, Suzuki opened the first Bincho location. The company name derives from binchotan (備長炭), the white oak charcoal central to the restaurant's cooking method; the logo's orange color represents glowing charcoal.
bio.origin: Aichi Prefecture, Japan (founded 1992; now 12 locations across Aichi, Tokyo, Osaka, and Fukuoka)
bio.specialty: Hitsumabushi — charcoal-grilled eel (unagi) served over rice in a wooden ohitsu vessel, eaten three ways: plain, with condiments (wasabi, negi, nori), and poured with dashi broth as ochazuke.
bio.philosophy: Freshly grilled eel (焼きたて) paired with freshly cooked rice (炊きたて): eel is grilled only after each order, and rice is cooked in batches of no more than 20 portions to guarantee peak texture. The brand's mission is preserving and disseminating the traditional culture of eel cuisine (鰻の伝統文化の伝承).

anecdotes.text: Suzuki learned Nagoya's distinctive jiyaki (地焼き) technique — direct charcoal grilling without the steaming step used in Kanto-style eel — during his 10-year apprenticeship at Unagi no Nishimoto. The method renders fat from the skin, yielding the restaurant's signature crispy exterior and tender interior.

The Kinshachi Yokocho branch sits in the Yoshinao Zone of the castle-side food district, and the menu includes a location-exclusive 金シャチひつまぶし (Gold Shachi Hitsumabushi) using 1.2 portions of eel — a nod to the golden shachihoko (dolphin-tiger) ornaments atop Nagoya Castle.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information is published about shared cooking surfaces or cross-contamination protocols. The open charcoal grill environment means multiple items may share preparation space.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house tare uses tamari soy sauce (たまり醤油), which is traditionally brewed with little or no wheat — unlike standard koikuchi soy sauce. However, Bincho has not publicly confirmed the specific tamari is wheat-free or carries any GF certification. Celiac diners must verify directly.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Not applicable — no vegan dishes on the menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu or published accommodation policy found across the official website, Kinshachi Yokocho listing, Nagoya tourism info, or third-party reservation platforms. Phone reservations are available (052-223-5777) and contacting the restaurant in advance is the only recommended path for allergy concerns.

positives.text: Core protein is plain eel, charcoal-grilled with no breading or tempura coating — minimal ingredient complexity compared to multi-component Japanese dishes.

caveats: Hours are irregular and subject to seasonal changes, Nagoya Castle closure schedule, and early closure when eel sells out. The Monday-closed rule applies on regular Mondays; if Monday is a public holiday, the closure falls on the following Tuesday. Weekend hours split into lunch and dinner sittings. Verify current hours before visiting.

cuisine: イタリアン（地産地消コース料理）
neighborhood: 東区白壁・尼ケ坂
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市東区白壁4-63-3
geocode_note: GSI geocode returns 136.918396, 35.183006, approximately 1.72 km straight-line from Nagoya Castle — marginally outside the 1.61 km check radius. However, the restaurant is definitively in 東区白壁 (the named target neighborhood) and Tabelog categorizes it under 尼ケ坂, which matches the area description exactly. The ~15-minute walk cited in the listing is consistent with this distance via city streets.
vegan_detail: Full Italian course menu built around fish and meat from named local producers — wagyu beef (Genji Wagyu), sea bream, sea bass, striped prawn, bottarga. No vegetarian or vegan course option mentioned on any source reviewed. Hidden fish elements are pervasive: bottarga, anchovy, seafood broths are core to the menu style.
gf_detail: Handmade pasta is a signature of the restaurant and the chef's craft identity — the official site explicitly states Japanese wheat flour is used for all pasta. No gluten-free pasta or gluten-free course alternative is mentioned anywhere. Standard Italian course format with pasta as a centerpiece makes celiac-safe dining not feasible without significant advance coordination, which is not offered or advertised.
hours_raw: ランチ 11:30〜14:00（最終入店12:00）、ディナー 18:00〜22:00（ラストオーダー20:00）。月曜定休＋不定休あり。
seats_text: 8席（テーブル席＋個室）。完全予約制で一斉スタートのコース形式。
cultural_comfort_note: Tiny 8-seat reservation-only space serving a fixed-time course in Japanese; prior reservation via Tabelog or phone (Japanese) is required, and the intimate format assumes guests can follow Japanese-language service.

bio.background: オーナーシェフ押川大仁は、恵比寿のリストランテMASSAで師匠・神戸勝彦のもとで修業し、「生産者との繋がりを大切にする」という哲学を受け継いだ。開店前に東海エリアの生産者を自ら訪ね歩き、信頼関係を築いてから2016年に白壁に独立開業した。
bio.origin: 名古屋市東区白壁（2016年開業）
bio.specialty: 手打ちパスタ（師から直伝）と東海地産の魚介・野菜・和牛を用いた日替わりコース料理
bio.philosophy: 「生産者の想いをお客様に伝えることが料理人の役目」をモットーに、無農薬・天然食材を最小限の味付けで仕上げ、素材本来の味を最大限に引き出す軽やかなイタリアン。メニューには生産者名・産地を明記する。

anecdotes.text: 店名「ヴィオレッタ」はスミレの花を意味し、その花言葉「小さなしあわせ」がコンセプトの根底にある。飛騨高山の木材を使った家具で温かみのある空間を演出している。
仕入れ先にはDOIMAナチュラルファーム（有機在来野菜）、鈴木農園（根菜）、源氏和牛（年間100頭の希少な和牛）、北加農場の放し飼い卵など、産地・生産者を特定した食材を使用。気温・湿度に合わせてパスタの製法を日々調整する。
2019年ミシュランガイド愛知・岐阜・三重でミシュランプレートを獲得。食べログイタリアンEAST百名店2025にも選出されている。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 手打ちパスタが料理の核心であり、小麦粉の使用は不可避。グルテンフリー対応の記述はいかなるソースにも見当たらない。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: イタリアン料理のため醤油は主要食材ではないが、素材の詳細な調理情報は非公開のため、調味料の小麦含有を個別に確認することが必要。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 魚介（カツオ・アンチョビ・ボタルガ等）と肉類（和牛）が全コースを通じて使用されており、植物性のみの調理は行われていない。

staff_allergy_handling.text: アレルギー対応・グルテンフリー・ヴィーガン対応についての記述はいかなるソース（公式サイト・食べログ・ブログ記事）にも確認できなかった。予約時に直接確認が必要。

caveats: Geocode places the restaurant at ~1.72km straight-line from Nagoya Castle, marginally outside the 1.61km check radius, but the restaurant is definitively within the 東区白壁・尼ケ坂 neighborhood named in the target area description. Hours shown are from the official site as of 2024–2025; the restaurant notes irregular closures in addition to Monday. Credit cards accepted only for courses priced ¥15,500+ (one card per table). Service charge of 10% added. Private room available with ¥500/person surcharge.

neighborhood: Josai, Nishi Ward — approx. 500m southwest of Nagoya Castle
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市西区城西2-1-6 ハイツSS 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 愛知県名古屋市西区城西二丁目１番６号 returned [136.894608, 35.184254]. Distance to Nagoya Castle (35.1856, 136.8997) is approximately 486m — well within the 1.61km target radius.
seats_text: 16 seats total: 2 counter seats and table seating
cultural_comfort_note: Fully reservation-only with online booking only (no phone calls accepted); signage and communication are in Japanese, so comfort with Japanese-language booking platforms is needed.
vegan_detail: This is a meat-focused yakiniku and horumon (offal) restaurant with course menus built around black wagyu beef. No plant-based or vegan options are offered.
gf_detail: Standard yakiniku tare (dipping sauce) and marinades in Japan typically contain wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu or allergen information is listed anywhere on Tabelog or the restaurant's Instagram. Dedicated GF accommodation is unconfirmed — those with celiac disease should enquire before booking about whether tamari or wheat-free alternatives are available. Cross-contamination risk from shared grilling surfaces is a further consideration.

bio.background: Opened in October 2025 in a compact 16-seat space on the first floor of Heights SS building in Josai, a quiet residential street a short walk southwest of Nagoya Castle. The restaurant operates on a fully reservation-only basis with online booking only.
bio.specialty: Signature wagyu course centred on black wagyu beef (黒毛和牛); the restaurant claims to be the originator of miso-tan (みそたん発祥の店), a miso-marinated beef tongue preparation.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's Instagram identifies it as the birthplace of miso-tan (みそたん発祥の店), a distinctive preparation of beef tongue finished with miso.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Shared charcoal grills are standard at yakiniku restaurants; no information about dedicated or separated grilling equipment for allergen-free cooking.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakiniku tare (dipping sauces) and marinades in Japan are typically made with wheat-containing soy sauce. No gluten-free soy sauce (tamari) alternative is mentioned anywhere in the restaurant's listings.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entirely meat-focused menu with no plant-based dishes; vegan cross-contact is not applicable as vegan dining is not possible here.

caveats: Restaurant opened October 2025 and has only 7 Tabelog reviews as of research date; chef/owner identity is not publicly documented. The miso-tan origin claim comes from the restaurant's own Instagram only. No gluten-free or allergen information is published anywhere — GF diners must enquire directly at the time of reservation. Hours shown cover Mon-Sat; public holidays that fall Mon-Sat are also closed (treated same as Sundays).

name_ja: 天ぷら かき揚げ 光村
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市北区清水3-19-18
geocode_note: GSI geocode returned coordinates for 愛知県名古屋市北区清水三丁目１９番１８号; 1.25 km from Nagoya Castle, within 1.61 km radius.
neighborhood: 北区清水（清水駅近く）
seats_text: 53席（1階：カウンター11席＋テーブル2卓、2階：テーブル席）。10名以上の個室あり（要予約）。
gf_detail: Traditional tempura is made with wheat-flour batter; no gluten-free batter or dedicated fryer documented. Soy-based dipping sauce (tentsuyu) almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce. Sesame oil is used for frying, but the frying oil is shared across all tempura and kakiage. No allergen menu, no staff allergy protocol, and no tamari or GF alternatives are mentioned on the official site or any reviewed source. Celiac diners should avoid.
vegan_detail: The kitchen centers on shrimp-heavy kakiage and other seafood tempura. Even vegetable tempura items are fried in the same oil as shrimp and fish. Dipping sauce (tentsuyu) is typically made with dashi from bonito/fish. No vegan-safe option or plant-based dashi confirmed. Vegans cannot safely eat here without direct confirmation from staff, which the restaurant does not facilitate (no English phone support confirmed).
cultural_comfort_note: The restaurant is a local neighbourhood institution; an English menu download is available on the official site, but the setting, ordering process, and staff interaction are primarily in Japanese.

bio.background: 光村は1973年（昭和48年）創業の天ぷら専門店。名古屋市北区清水の住宅街に根を張り、半世紀以上にわたって地元客に愛されてきた老舗。2019年ミシュランガイド愛知・岐阜・三重版でミシュランプレートを獲得し、2025年には食べログ 天ぷら百名店にも選出されている。
bio.origin: 名古屋市北区清水（1973年創業）
bio.specialty: かき揚げ丼。ぷりぷりの小海老をふんだんに使ったかき揚げは丼から溢れんばかりの肉厚さで、甘辛のたれとごま油の香りが特徴。特上かき揚げ丼は海老の量が約2倍。
bio.philosophy: 食材がなくなり次第終了という営業スタイルが示すとおり、鮮度と素材本位を重視する姿勢を一貫している。

anecdotes.text: 昼の開店前から行列ができるほどの人気店で、テーブルの順番ではなく注文順に料理が提供されるという独自のシステムを採用している。
2026年7月1日より原材料の急激なコスト上昇を理由に価格改定を予定していることを公式サイトで告知しており、長年据え置いてきた価格への真摯な姿勢が伝わる。

gf_cross_contamination.text: All tempura and kakiage — including vegetable items — are fried in the same sesame oil. No dedicated fryer for any allergen category is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tentsuyu dipping sauce and bowl tare are standard Japanese preparations that almost certainly contain wheat-based soy sauce; no tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable tempura items share the frying oil with shrimp and fish tempura; no separation of cooking surfaces or oil confirmed.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, written allergy protocol, or English-language inquiry pathway has been documented on the official site or any third-party source reviewed.

positives.text: Sesame oil is used for frying (not blended vegetable shortening), which some diners prefer for its distinct flavour profile and lower prevalence of certain seed allergens.

caveats: Hours marked irregular because the restaurant closes on both Wednesdays AND the 1st and 3rd Tuesdays of each month, and may close early when ingredients run out. Seat count (53) is from Tabelog listing; Gnavi shows 64 — the Tabelog figure is used as primary. A price increase is scheduled for July 2026. Lunch reservations are accepted only for wheelchair users; weekend/holiday dinner requires a minimum party of 3.

name_ja: 郷土素材料理 山人昔楼
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市東区白壁3-26-23
geocode_note: GSI address-search resolved to Shirakabe 3-chome level (35.185421, 136.914825), approximately 1,377m from Nagoya Castle — within the 1.61km target radius.
neighborhood: 東区白壁・尼ケ坂
seats_text: 42席（個室あり）。カウンター席と個室を備え、全席から庭の緑を望める。
cultural_comfort_note: Traditional kappo setting with tatami-style private rooms; staff are hospitable but the environment is formal Japanese dining — no English menus confirmed, and advance reservations are required.
vegan_detail: Standard kappo menu built around meat, fish, and seafood. Nearly all dishes incorporate fish-based dashi, soy sauce, or miso. No dedicated vegan options are listed. Allergy accommodation is possible with advance notice, but vegan requests would require explicit communication at booking time — not guaranteed.
gf_detail: Wheat is pervasive: soy sauce and miso appear throughout, tempura batter is used in multiple dishes, and rice/noodle courses include soy-glazed preparations. No gluten-free menu or dedicated GF accommodation is advertised. The restaurant states allergies can be addressed with advance notice, but there is no evidence of celiac-safe protocols or a dedicated fryer. Celiacs should contact the restaurant directly before booking and ask specifically about wheat-free alternatives to soy sauce (tamari) and tempura dishes.

bio.background: A 70-year-old traditional private residence adjacent to a small park in Nagoya's Shirakabe district was carefully renovated and opened as a restaurant in November 2005. The building underwent a further major renovation and reopened in October 2025. The location is a 1-minute walk from Amagasaka Station, tucked into a quiet residential streetscape.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled seasonal ingredients: single-night-dried fish sourced from around Japan, three-variety Japanese heritage chicken (Akita Hinai, Nagoya Cochin, Satsuma Jidori), Kumamoto horse-meat sashimi, and wagyu loin grilled tableside over bincho charcoal.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen's guiding principle is simplicity in service of the ingredient: boiling, grilling, and steaming are used to bring out natural flavors, with ancient salt and aged soy sauce as seasonings, and bincho charcoal for all grilled items. The motto is to let carefully sourced ingredients speak for themselves.

anecdotes.text: Head chef Fukute Haruhiko works at the open counter, grilling directly-sourced overnight-dried fish over live bincho charcoal within view of guests — a deliberate choice that makes the craft of the kitchen part of the dining experience.

The dining room is designed so that every seat — counter, private room, and upstairs — faces the Japanese garden. A glass floor section in one room has garden stones embedded beneath it, blurring the line between interior and exterior.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura appears in multiple set courses and à la carte dishes. No information on a dedicated fryer or separation from other fried items.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Aged soy sauce (熟成醤油) is a stated house seasoning used across grilled, simmered, and rice dishes. Miso-based preparations also appear throughout the menu. Both contain wheat.
Charcoal-grilled rice balls with shoyu glaze and soy-glazed bamboo shoots are among listed dishes — standard wheat-containing soy sauce is used.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi is a foundational ingredient in Japanese kappo cooking and is almost certainly present in soups and simmered dishes even where not explicitly listed.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Multiple sources report that the restaurant accommodates allergy requests when notified in advance. No formal allergy protocol or written allergen menu is documented.

positives.text: The kitchen uses high-quality ancient salt (古代塩) as a primary seasoning alongside soy sauce, meaning some simply-salted grilled items may be free of soy sauce and wheat — worth asking about specifically.

caveats: Geocode resolved to chō-level (Shirakabe 3-chome) rather than the exact building number; actual coordinates may differ slightly. Distance from Nagoya Castle is estimated at ~1,377m based on chō-level geocode. GF and vegan assessments are based on menu analysis and general kappo conventions — no direct staff confirmation was obtained. Hours reflect Tabelog data as of mid-2026; the restaurant noted schedule changes (weekday lunch discontinued except for groups 10+) so confirm before visiting.

address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市西区児玉2-4-13
geocode_note: GSI geocode returned coordinates for 愛知県名古屋市西区児玉二丁目４番１３号. Distance to Nagoya Castle target (35.1856, 136.8997) is approximately 1,591m — within the 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 西区児玉 (浄心駅徒歩約8〜10分)
seats_text: 10 seats total: 6 counter seats and a 4-seat tatami room. The counter is the heart of the experience.
cultural_comfort_note: No English signage or menu; the chef is famously quiet and interaction flows primarily through his wife who manages hospitality — comfortable for Japanese-speaking diners, challenging for non-Japanese speakers.
vegan_detail: Traditional kappo cuisine built on fish-based dashi (the chef intentionally avoids kombu dashi, preferring fish stock) and seasonal seafood as central ingredients. No vegan alternatives are offered. Signature dishes include shrimp tempura tenmusu and dashimaki tamago.
gf_detail: Wheat is present throughout: tempura batter (signature tenmusu), soy sauce (used in seasoning), kitsune udon (wheat noodles), and dashimaki tamago. No gluten-free menu or substitution system is documented anywhere. Given the reservation-only, fully-booked format, diners with celiac should contact the restaurant far in advance — though no evidence exists that GF accommodations have been made.

bio.origin: 愛知県碧南市 (Hekinan, Aichi Prefecture)
bio.background: Born in Hekinan, Aichi, Ginzo Hanaichi left home at 16 for Osaka to apprentice in kappo and sushi restaurants. At 29 he returned to Nagoya and in 1983 opened 花いち in a quiet residential street in Nishi Ward — a converted traditional house with no exterior signage. Over 40-plus years he has maintained the kappo format unchanged, cooking entirely from scratch before guests each evening.
bio.specialty: Tenmusu (shrimp tempura rice ball) and freshly fried hanpen (fish cake with burdock) — dishes that distill the chef's obsession with serving food at the precise moment it is done.
bio.philosophy: The chef coined the phrase 不触不味 — roughly, 'do not touch what you would ruin by touching' — meaning the less you manipulate an ingredient the truer its flavor. He avoids kombu dashi entirely, believing it overwhelms seasonal produce. Nothing is made in advance; every dish is cooked to order.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant has no sign on the door. A single wooden board bearing the brushed characters 不触不味 hangs under the eaves — that is the only outward mark of one of Nagoya's most-awarded restaurants.

Regulars joke that the chef's voice is heard so rarely it is practically a menu item: '会話なら料理として' — 'if you want conversation, order it as a dish.' His wife Toshie manages all hospitality while he focuses entirely on the counter.

The daily handwritten menu is numbered by the restaurant's operating days — one visitor noted they were on day 9,603. After 40 years, the counter seats and the menu board look exactly as they did when 花いち opened in 1983.
As of the time of writing, new reservations are not being accepted; the restaurant is reportedly booked more than two years ahead.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura (shrimp, squid, dried potato) is fried on the counter in front of guests — a shared cooking environment where wheat-containing tempura batter is in active use throughout service.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-containing soy sauce is used in seasoning. No tamari or GF soy sauce substitution is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi underpins most preparations. The chef explicitly avoids kombu dashi in favour of fish stock, making an all-plant meal structurally incompatible with the kitchen's approach.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, substitution policy, or allergy-handling protocol is documented in any reviewed source. Given the reservation-only format booked years in advance, allergen requests would need to be raised at the time of booking.

positives.text: Every dish is cooked entirely from scratch to order — no advance preparation or pre-made sauces — which means the chef has full visibility of what goes into each dish at each moment of service.

caveats: Reservation situation: as of 2025–2026 sources, new reservations are not being accepted and the wait is reported at 2+ years. Hours confirmed from multiple sources as Mon/Tue/Thu–Sun 17:00–22:00, closed Wednesday and public holidays. GF and vegan safety ratings are necessarily conservative (ask / no) as no accommodation data exists in any source reviewed — the omakase-style menu rotates daily around seasonal fish and wheat-containing preparations. Cash only confirmed across multiple platforms.

neighborhood: 国際センター・那古野（円頓寺商店街）
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市西区那古野2-20-22 2F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 那古野2-20-22; coordinates are within 1.36 km of Nagoya Castle, inside the 1.61 km target radius.
vegan_detail: Entirely chicken-focused omakase. Every course centers on binchotan-grilled heritage chicken skewers. No plant-based alternative course is offered.
gf_detail: Standard yakitori tare is made with wheat-containing soy sauce. Salt-grilled (shio) skewers are the lower-risk option but the same grill handles tare-glazed pieces, creating cross-contamination risk. No dedicated gluten-free facility or certified GF menu was found. Celiac diners should call ahead; the intimate 8-seat counter format may allow chef accommodation, but nothing is confirmed.
seats_text: 8-seat counter only; very small and intimate.
cultural_comfort_note: An intimate 8-seat counter where conversation with the chef is part of the experience; basic Japanese will help, though the warm, approachable atmosphere is noted by regulars.

bio.background: Opened in June 2023 on the second floor of a building in the historic Entoji shopping street (円頓寺商店街) district of Nagoya's Nishi Ward. The restaurant occupies a small but stylish space, part of the Kinsyachi Shoutengai merchant collective.
bio.specialty: Binchotan charcoal-grilled heritage chicken skewers — the kitchen centers on premium female Omi chicken (淡海地鶏メス) along with Amakusa Daio (天草大王), sourced for flavor and breed integrity.
bio.philosophy: Precision over quantity: an intimate omakase-style course built around the quality of the bird, the exactness of the heat, and the seasoning balance. Natural wines and local sake are chosen to complement, not overshadow, the grill.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers note the chef's focus on female Omi chicken — a deliberate sourcing decision that sets the skewers apart from standard yakitori. One regular wrote that the heat and salt balance was exactly right across every skewer.
The shop fills almost entirely with repeat customers, a sign of how quickly a loyal clientele formed around the 8-seat counter since its June 2023 opening.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tare-glazed and shio (salt) skewers share the same binchotan grill. Cross-contamination from wheat-based tare drips or brush contact is likely for celiac-risk diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakitori tare is traditionally made with soy sauce (containing wheat). No gluten-free soy sauce alternative was mentioned anywhere in available sources.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is built around grilled chicken; there is no plant-based course and vegan cross-contact is near-certain.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling policy or staff training information is publicly documented. The 8-seat counter format does allow direct communication with the chef, which may make individual requests possible — call ahead.

positives.text: Shio (salt-only) skewers are available and may avoid direct tare/wheat contact for lower-sensitivity guests, though shared grill risk remains.

caveats: Hours from kinsyachi.com (金シャチ商店街 official directory) show 17:00 open; Tabelog lists 18:00 — slight discrepancy, call to confirm. Under-30 status unknown (opened 2023, owner age not found). No allergy policy data available; dietary fields reflect general yakitori category knowledge rather than confirmed shop-specific statements.

name_ja: スパイシーカレー クスクス 白壁店
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市東区白壁2-1-28 法令堂ビル
geocode_note: GSI geocode on 愛知県名古屋市東区白壁二丁目１番２８号 returned [136.909576, 35.183037]. 942m from Nagoya Castle — within the 1.61km target radius.
neighborhood: 東大手・白壁 (Higashi Ward)
cuisine: インド・ネパールカレー (Indian-Nepalese curry)
seats_text: 40 seats across counter, table, and sofa seating
closed_days: No regular closed day listed; year-end holidays only
cultural_comfort_note: Nepalese-run neighborhood curry shop near Nagoya Castle; menu is accessible and set meals are easy to order, though staff likely communicate primarily in Japanese.
vegan_detail: No vegan designation on the menu. Curries are likely made with ghee or cream in some preparations (Malai Tikka, butter-based sauces). A rice-based set without dairy toppings may be achievable but would require direct confirmation with staff. The spice-fried chicken and kebabs contain meat; no plant-based curry option is advertised.
gf_detail: No gluten-free designation anywhere. Naan is wheat-based and the default bread; basmati rice is available as an alternative and is naturally GF. Indian/Nepalese-style curry sauces are not typically thickened with wheat flour, but cross-contamination from naan baking in a shared kitchen is a realistic risk. No dedicated fryer or allergen protocol is documented. Celiac diners should call ahead and confirm rice-only ordering, shared cooking surfaces, and any wheat-containing marinades or sauces.

bio.background: The Shirakabe branch is the second location of a well-regarded Nagoya curry operation that traces its roots to a shop called Kushukusu (クシュクシュ) in Midori Ward's Takino neighborhood. After closing and relocating, the concept was reborn as Kusukusu at a Nonami (Tenpaku Ward) address, building a loyal following before opening this Shirakabe outpost in January 2024 — a 1-minute walk from Higashi-Ote Station and well within walking distance of Nagoya Castle.
bio.specialty: Malwani curry (マルワニカレー) — a rare Western Indian preparation combining South and East Indian spice techniques; described by reviewers as a thin, aromatic, broth-like sauce that builds heat gradually
bio.philosophy: Authentic India-Nepal spice cooking aimed at Japanese palates, balancing acidity, spice complexity, and sweetness rather than raw heat alone. Monthly limited specials keep the menu rotating.

anecdotes.text: A Nagoya food blog noted the signage reads 'Indo-Nepal Spicy Curry' and praised the marwani curry as one of the few places in Japan serving this regional Western Indian dish with genuine technique — the curry is described as 'a thin ocean of sauce with rice as an island.'

The nagoya-meshi.com review calls the restaurant 'eight-faced, six-armed' (八面六臂) — a Japanese idiom for remarkably versatile — in describing how the kitchen handles everything from fragrant spice-fried chicken to creamy tikka to this unusual marwani preparation.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Naan (wheat) is baked on-premises and is the default bread; the kitchen handles wheat continuously. No dedicated GF prep area or protocol is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Indian-Nepalese curry cuisine does not typically use soy sauce; no soy sauce is mentioned in any source reviewed. However, no allergen statement has been published by the restaurant, so this cannot be guaranteed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Ghee, yogurt, and cream are standard in multiple menu items (Malai Tikka, butter-based preparations). A shared kitchen serving meat and dairy means cross-contact for vegan diners is probable without explicit accommodation.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling policy or allergen menu has been published. Phone reservations are noted as unavailable (online booking only), making pre-visit allergy communication more difficult — diners should plan to ask at the counter on arrival.

positives.text: Basmati rice is available as a wheat-free alternative to naan and can be ordered as part of the set meal, offering a naturally gluten-free carb base.

caveats: No allergen menu, no GF/vegan labeling, and no documented allergy protocol. Phone reservations are disabled; allergy questions must be raised in person. Hours and closures should be verified before visiting as the restaurant is relatively new (opened January 2024).

cuisine: きしめん・名古屋めし
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市中区本丸1-1 名古屋城内
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 名古屋市中区本丸１番; coordinates place the restaurant inside Nagoya Castle grounds, approximately 229m from the target center — well within the 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 中区本丸（名古屋城内）/ 名古屋城駅
seats_text: 28 seats; self-service counter-style ordering via multilingual touchscreen ticket vending machine (Japanese, English, Chinese, Korean). Tray your meal and find a table.
hours_raw: 10:00–16:00 (last order around 16:15–16:30 per some sources); closed Dec 29–31
closed_days: December 29–31 (aligned with Nagoya Castle closure); otherwise open daily including weekends and national holidays. Castle itself has occasional irregular closures — check castle hours before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: The ticket vending machine supports English, Chinese, and Korean, making ordering straightforward for non-Japanese speakers, though staff interaction beyond order pickup is minimal.
vegan_detail: No vegan options. All kishimen dishes are made with a rich bonito (katsuo) and fish-based dashi broth. Standard toppings include bonito flakes, fried tofu (aburaage, which may share fryer oil), and wakame. No plant-based broth alternative is available. Set meals include inari sushi (which contains dashi-seasoned rice) and miso-breaded cutlets with pork.
gf_detail: Kishimen is a flat wheat noodle — gluten is the core ingredient and cannot be substituted. The soy sauce used in the dashi broth also contains wheat. There is no gluten-free alternative on the menu. Deep-fried items (tempura, kushi-age) share the kitchen environment. This restaurant is not suitable for anyone with celiac disease or serious gluten intolerance.

bio.background: Nagoyajo Kishimen-tei has operated inside Nagoya Castle since 1972 (Showa 47), run by the Aichi Prefectural Noodle Association (愛知県めんるい組合), the trade body representing the region's udon and soba makers. The association holds the concession to serve noodles on the castle grounds, and this shop is cited as its only official restaurant within the castle.
bio.specialty: Nagoya-style kishimen — wide, flat wheat noodles in a deep bonito-and-soy dashi, topped with wakame seaweed, sweet aburaage fried tofu, and bonito flakes. The classic warm bowl (名物きしめん) starts at ¥550; a cold zaru-style version (きしころ) is available in warmer months.
bio.philosophy: Simple, honest noodle-stand cooking rooted in Nagoya's kishimen tradition, served to castle visitors at accessible prices in an open, self-service format.
roles: Aichi Prefectural Noodle Association (愛知県めんるい組合) — operator since 1972

anecdotes.text: One local food blog reports that the Nagoya mayor reportedly visits the stall incognito to eat kishimen — a claim the blog treats as an open secret among regulars.
The restaurant pioneered a modern touch-screen ticket vending machine with menus in English, Chinese, and Korean — a notably high-tech touch for a no-frills castle noodle stand.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kishimen noodles are made from wheat flour — gluten is the primary ingredient. No GF alternative exists. The kitchen also prepares tempura and fried items in the same space.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dashi broth is seasoned with standard soy sauce, which contains wheat. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce option is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bonito flakes (katsuobushi) are a direct topping on kishimen and are also used in the broth base. There is no plant-based broth option. All menu items contain animal-derived ingredients.

caveats: Visiting requires purchasing a Nagoya Castle admission ticket (¥500 adults). Hours may vary with castle events or seasonal programs — confirm on the castle's official site before visiting. Last order time varies by source (16:00–16:30); arriving by 15:45 is safest. QR code payment (PayPay) accepted; credit cards and IC cards are not.

neighborhood: 丸の内／久屋大通
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市中区丸の内3-17-29 丸の内iaビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode returned coordinates for 愛知県名古屋市中区丸の内三丁目１７番２９号; confirmed within target box (lat 35.175, lng 136.905). Distance to Nagoya Castle approx 1.26km — within 1.61km radius.
seats_text: 14席（カウンター10席、個室1室）
cultural_comfort_note: Small counter-only kaiseki restaurant where Japanese is the working language; no English materials or bilingual staff noted, and the intimate 14-seat format assumes familiarity with kaiseki etiquette.
gf_detail: Traditional kaiseki format with high likelihood of wheat in standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu), and fish-based dashi almost certain throughout courses. Red miso soup is listed as part of every course. No gluten-free menu or dedicated accommodations are advertised. The website says to notify of allergies in advance, but makes no GF-safe promises. Cross-contamination risk in a small kitchen producing omakase courses is real. Celiacs should call ahead and treat this as "ask" at best.
vegan_detail: Full-course kaiseki centred on premium seafood (sashimi, fish dishes) and A5 wagyu beef, with courses including miso soup and dashi-based dishes. No vegan course or plant-based alternative is listed. Hidden animal-derived dashi (katsuo/bonito or niboshi) is standard in this style of cooking. Vegans should inquire well in advance — significant menu restructuring would be required and is not guaranteed.

bio.background: Born 1969 in Aichi Prefecture and raised in a washoku chef's household, Nagashima has spent over 40 years in Japanese cuisine. He has been visiting Nagoya's Yanagibashi Market daily for more than 30 years, building relationships with vendors and selecting fish, produce, and premium beef by eye each morning. He has also made personal visits to California wine producers — particularly Beringer — to curate the restaurant's wine list himself.
bio.origin: The restaurant originally operated under the name 円居 (Enkyo) for around 20 years before relocating and reopening under the current name なごや えんきょ in the Marunouchi ia Building.
bio.specialty: Omakase kaiseki courses built around whatever Nagashima considers finest at market that day — sashimi, seasonal fish dishes, A5 wagyu, and clay-pot rice are recurring highlights. Black truffle rice is noted as a signature item.
bio.philosophy: Bringing out the original deliciousness of seasonal ingredients through careful sourcing and creative refinement, rather than heavy elaboration. The philosophy pairs Japanese culinary tradition with an adventurous approach to wine, unusual for this genre.

anecdotes.text: Nagashima has visited Yanagibashi Market every single day for over 30 years, selecting ingredients personally — a practice he describes as the foundation of the restaurant's quality.

To build the wine list, Nagashima has travelled to California wineries, including Beringer, to taste and select bottles directly — a rare cross-cultural commitment for a traditional kaiseki-style restaurant.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free kitchen or cross-contamination controls are described. Omakase production in a compact kitchen handling soy sauce, miso, and breaded or battered items creates real shared-surface risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (containing wheat) is almost certainly used throughout. No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere on the official site or third-party listings.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All courses include fish-based dashi (red dashi miso soup confirmed), sashimi, and meat. Animal products are integral to every course component; no plant-only preparation line exists.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The menu page asks guests to notify the restaurant of allergies in advance, but no specific allergy protocol, substitution policy, or staff training is described.

positives.text: Advance notice is explicitly requested for allergies, and the intimate counter format means the single chef oversees all preparation — reducing the risk of miscommunication between kitchen staff compared to large restaurants.

caveats: Hours for Sunday reflect "closed" as the listed default; the restaurant notes it can accommodate Sunday/holiday bookings by prior arrangement only. Seat count of 14 includes 10 counter seats plus a private room that accommodates the remainder. Chef name sourced from Hitosara profile — not confirmed on official site directly. No gluten-free or vegan accommodation details are publicly documented; all dietary safety ratings are conservative estimates based on cuisine type.

address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市西区上名古屋1-2-17
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI address search for 愛知県名古屋市西区上名古屋一丁目２番１７号; coordinates fall within the Nishi Ward target box and approximately 0.85 km from Nagoya Castle, well inside the 1.61 km radius.
neighborhood: 西区 上名古屋 (Kaminagoya), Nishi Ward
seats_text: 4 counter seats
hours_raw: 営業日はinstagram参照 (operating days announced via Instagram @hoshinoko_shokudou)
closed_days: Not fixed; irregular schedule posted to Instagram
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny solo-run counter with no English signage or menu; photography of food, interior, and chef is prohibited, suggesting an intimate local atmosphere where basic Japanese or pointing at the day's dishes is the expected way to order.
vegan_detail: The menu is traditional Japanese canteen cooking — seasonal bento boxes, simmered side dishes, and baked goods. Standard Japanese ingredients such as dashi (likely fish-based), katsuo (bonito), and egg are almost certainly present in many items. No vegan or plant-based menu has been advertised. Ask directly on the day; some vegetable side dishes may incidentally be vegan, but this cannot be assumed.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or allergen information is published. Standard Japanese cooking relies heavily on wheat-containing soy sauce (shoyu), and baked goods on the menu (butter cookies, pound cake, bolo) contain wheat flour. One source notes the owner uses naturally fermented soy sauce, but whether tamari or GF alternatives are available is unknown. The kitchen is a tiny single-operator counter with no dedicated fryer information available. Celiacs should contact the restaurant in advance via Instagram; do not assume safety.

bio.background: Miyata Seiko, born 1979, trained at a culinary academy straight out of high school before spending years working in welfare and care facilities. The hands-on experience feeding vulnerable people in medical and social-care settings convinced her to pursue the highest nutritional credential: she left her job in her late twenties, enrolled in a nutritionist academy, and passed the national registered dietitian exam. She holds both a cooking chef license and a registered dietitian qualification.
bio.specialty: Seasonal Japanese bento boxes and simple baked sweets — including lemon cookies, butter cookies, and pound cake — made with consciously sourced ingredients including naturally fermented soy sauce.
bio.philosophy: Everyday food that people actually want to eat every day: unpretentious, body-conscious, and ingredient-honest. The menu rotates with the season and keeps flavour and restraint in balance.

anecdotes.text: At Nagoya's monthly Higashi Betsuin morning market (the 28th of each month), Hoshinoko Shokudo regularly drew queues of 20 or more before the stall even opened — regulars learned to arrive early or miss out.
The shop opened a fixed 4-seat counter location in Nishi Ward in January 2026, a step up from years of market and event pop-ups that took it as far as the Tokyo and Kansai Nomi-no-Ichi flea markets.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is a tiny 4-seat solo-run counter with no information published about cross-contamination controls; wheat flour is used in baked goods on the menu, making cross-contact plausible.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese cooking is the base; soy sauce (wheat-containing shoyu) is almost certainly used in simmered dishes and bento items. One source notes naturally fermented soy sauce is used, but no tamari or wheat-free alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi and egg are standard in Japanese canteen cooking of this style; no plant-based or vegan-separated preparation has been mentioned.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling protocol is published. The owner is a registered dietitian, so ingredient awareness is high, but no formal allergen accommodation system has been publicly described.

positives.text: Owner holds registered dietitian and culinary chef qualifications, suggesting strong ingredient awareness and nutritional care in menu planning.
A focus on conscientious sourcing and body-conscious ingredients has been a consistent part of the brand's identity since its market-stall days.

caveats: Operating days are announced irregularly via Instagram only — always check @hoshinoko_shokudou before visiting. The shop opened this fixed location in January 2026; earlier references to コウノミBASE (香呑町4-90, near Shonai-dori) describe a previous address. Photography of food, interior, and chef is explicitly prohibited. No dietary or allergen accommodations are publicly described; the registered dietitian qualification of the owner does not substitute for formal allergy protocols.

cuisine: Izakaya / Taiwanese mixed noodles / Hakata tonkotsu ramen
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市西区花の木1-9-13
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 愛知県名古屋市西区花の木一丁目９番１３号 returned [136.89032, 35.186726], approximately 830m from the Nagoya Castle target center — well within the 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: Sengen Cho (浅間町), Nishi Ward
seats_text: 21 counter seats only; no table or private room seating.
cultural_comfort_note: Counter-only izakaya with no English signage or menu; reservations are typically made by phone in Japanese, and the small intimate counter means direct interaction with the chef in Japanese is expected.
vegan_detail: This is a traditional izakaya with a pork-forward menu — signature dishes are tonkotsu (pork bone) ramen and Taiwan mazesoba with minced pork. No vegan options are available or implied. Animal products are central to every signature dish.
gf_detail: The entire menu is built around wheat: ramen noodles (sourced from Fukuoka), wheat-based soy sauce in the house tare and custom oil blend, and the Taiwan mazesoba sauce. The review also notes tempura and Chinese dishes on the menu. No gluten-free options are available, no dedicated fryer information found, and there is no indication of any allergy accommodation. Cross-contamination risk is high in this small counter kitchen.

bio.background: Chef Kuwano Toshiaki grew up near Kurume in Fukuoka, the birthplace of Hakata tonkotsu ramen, and brought that pedigree to this compact neighborhood izakaya in Nagoya's Nishi Ward. The restaurant's thin Kyushu-sourced noodles are imported directly from the chef's home region.
bio.specialty: 台湾まぜそば (Taiwan mazesoba) — a dish that emerged by accident when regulars asked to add noodles to the popular Taiwan bean sprout stir-fry side dish. The result: thin Kyushu ramen noodles tossed with a secret soy-based tare, a proprietary three-oil blend, minced pork, big-cut garlic, chili peppers, bean sprouts, and Chinese chives, all wok-fired at high heat for a crispy finish. Priced at ¥800.
bio.philosophy: Authentic, generous, and deeply flavored — the chef has stated he will continue sourcing noodles from Kyushu for as long as he can maintain his standards.

anecdotes.text: The Taiwan mazesoba was never planned. It grew organically out of customer requests to put noodles in the popular Taiwan bean sprout stir-fry, and became the dish that now books the restaurant a month in advance.
At least one diner drove 1.5 hours from Gifu Prefecture after waiting 1.5 months for a reservation — the kind of pilgrimage this 21-seat counter has become known for.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free prep area or fryer has been identified. The kitchen operates as a small open counter and cooks ramen noodles, wheat-based sauces, and other wheat-containing dishes simultaneously. Cross-contamination risk should be assumed high.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The Taiwan mazesoba uses a house-made soy-based tare as its primary seasoning. Standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pork (minced, in broth) and other animal products are present in every dish. The tonkotsu ramen broth is pork-bone based. No vegan-safe preparation is possible at this restaurant.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling protocol, allergen menu, or dietary accommodation was found in any source. The restaurant has no English materials. Diners with serious food allergies should contact the restaurant directly by phone (052-524-0498) in Japanese before visiting.

caveats: Hours confirmed as Tue-Sat 17:30-22:00 (LO 21:00) across multiple sources; one search result cited 17:30-24:00 but this appears to be outdated — the Tabelog listing, tabemaro, and CBC Magazine all agree on 22:00 close with 21:00 last order. Cash only but PayPay QR accepted. Reservations book approximately one month out; walk-ins are unlikely to be seated. Smoking permitted at all seats.

name_ja: ガーブ カステッロ
cuisine: ピッツァ・イタリアン
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市北区名城1-4-1 名城公園内 tonarino 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode returned 愛知県名古屋市北区名城一丁目; coordinates place it ~420m from Nagoya Castle, well within the 1.61km target radius.
neighborhood: 北区・名城公園
seats_text: 230席（1F室内70席、1Fテラス30席、2F室内80席、2Fテラス50席）、ペット同伴はテラスのみ可
cultural_comfort_note: Large park-facing restaurant run by a listed Tokyo restaurant group; menus in Japanese but staff at a popular tourist-adjacent venue are accustomed to non-Japanese guests.
vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu or labelling found. Italian menu includes pasta, pizza, meat, and fish; dairy and eggs appear throughout. Some vegetable-focused antipasti and pizza variants may exist but no explicit vegan options are published. Guests would need to ask staff about preparation specifics.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or allergen chart is published. The kitchen centres on wood-fired Neapolitan pizza (wheat dough) and Italian pasta — wheat is present throughout the kitchen. No dedicated GF fryer or GF pizza base is mentioned anywhere. Soy sauce use is not confirmed but typical Italian preparations may employ wheat-containing condiments. Celiacs should contact the restaurant before visiting; safe options cannot be assumed.
caveats: No allergen menu or GF/vegan labelling was found on any source. This is a large corporate restaurant (Balnibarbi group) with wheat-heavy cuisine; it is not suitable as a dedicated GF or vegan recommendation. Hours exclude year-end; seasonal menus may change. Parking: 60 spaces, 1 hour complimentary with ¥5,000+ spend.

bio.background: GARB CASTELLO opened on April 27, 2017, as the anchor dining venue of tonarino, a commercial facility built within Nagoya's Meijo Park directly adjacent to Nagoya Castle. It is operated by Balnibarbi Corporation, a Osaka- and Tokyo-based restaurant group founded in 1991 and listed on the Tokyo Growth Exchange, known for developing large-scale lifestyle restaurants in park and waterfront settings across Japan.
bio.specialty: 薪釜ピッツァ (wood-fired Neapolitan pizza) baked in a live-fire oven visible from counter seating, alongside a broad Italian menu of pasta, meat, and fish dishes.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant's guiding idea is providing 'healing time amid greenery' — a relaxed, nature-immersed setting where the park landscape and views of Nagoya Castle become part of the dining experience. The space is designed for families, couples, and large groups alike, with a kids' play corner, pet-friendly terrace, year-round outdoor BBQ, and capacity for private events up to 230 guests.
bio.origin: 名古屋市北区名城公園 (Meijo Park, Kita Ward, Nagoya)

anecdotes.text: The tonarino complex where GARB CASTELLO sits was developed under the concept 'Shiro no tonari, anata no tonari' (next to the castle, next to you), positioning the dining venue as an everyday gathering place for both park visitors and local residents.
The restaurant offers year-round terrace BBQ at three tiered plans (¥3,000–¥5,000 per person), making it one of the few park-side BBQ destinations in central Nagoya.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is built around wheat-based pizza dough and pasta; cross-contamination with gluten must be assumed throughout. No allergen separation or dedicated preparation area is mentioned in any source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No information on soy sauce use is published. Italian kitchens may use wheat-containing condiments and sauces; cannot be confirmed or ruled out without contacting the restaurant.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy (cheese, cream) and eggs appear throughout the pizza and pasta menu. No vegan-separated preparation is indicated.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy-handling protocol, or staff training information is published on the official site or any third-party source reviewed.

address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市西区花の木2-9-6 ロイヤルヒルズ花の木1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch on 愛知県名古屋市西区花の木二丁目９番６号. Coordinates 35.188339, 136.886841. Distance to target (Nagoya Castle 35.1856, 136.8997) is approximately 1,208m — within the 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: Jōshin / Hananoki, Nishi Ward
seats_text: 5 counter seats, 16 tatami (sunken kotatsu-style)
hours_raw: Mon–Sat 11:30–14:00 (L.O. 14:00) / 17:30–22:00 (L.O. 21:30); Sunday closed (open on public holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional neighborhood noodle shop with a daily-board menu written in Japanese; no English signage or menus expected, but the young owner is noted as friendly and approachable.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu exists. The kitchen uses bonito-based dashi in noodle broths, and the menu includes chicken katsu, dashi-maki tamago, and tempura dishes. Plant-based diners would need to ask carefully about every item; a safe order is unlikely without advance communication.
gf_detail: The restaurant serves both soba and udon, with noodles described as pure handmade (純手打ち). Japanese soba at most small shops is a blended ratio — typically 80/20 or 70/30 buckwheat-to-wheat — rather than 100% buckwheat (juwari). No 100% buckwheat claim is made anywhere in available sources. Udon is entirely wheat-based. The kitchen also prepares tempura and chicken katsu, creating significant cross-contamination risk. There is no dedicated gluten-free preparation noted. Celiacs should avoid; those with mild sensitivity should call ahead.

bio.background: A compact, traditional noodle shop a short walk from Joshin Station in Nagoya's Nishi Ward, run by a young proprietor. The shop occupies the ground floor of a residential building and seats just 21 people across a counter and a tatami room with sunken seating, giving it the feel of a neighbourhood canteen rather than a formal restaurant.
bio.specialty: Handmade udon and soba (純手打ちうどん・そば), with a rotating daily-board menu. Lunch draws locals for set meals around ¥780; evenings shift toward small plates and drinks. The house-blend soy sauce chicken katsu bowl (自家製醤油のチキンカツ丼) is a noted signature.
bio.philosophy: Volume-forward, everyday cooking using fresh ingredients, with menu variety that changes based on what is good that day.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe the atmosphere as feeling like coming home (ただいまと言いたくなる), and the young owner is consistently praised for a warm, welcoming manner.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura and breaded katsu are prepared in the same kitchen. No separate fryer or preparation area for gluten-free orders is mentioned anywhere in available sources.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes use a house-blend soy sauce (自家製醤油) for items like the chicken katsu bowl; conventional Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are described as using bonito-based dashi (かつお出汁). Tempura and dashi-maki tamago (egg) are prominent menu items, making a plant-free meal unlikely without special arrangement.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy protocol, or staff training for dietary restrictions is noted in any available source. The restaurant operates without credit card machines and with a chalkboard-only menu, suggesting a basic neighbourhood setup without formal allergen systems.

positives.text: The young owner is consistently described as approachable and communicative — a direct phone call (052-524-6555) ahead of a visit is the most practical path for anyone with dietary needs.

caveats: No official website exists. Hours differ slightly across sources (lunch L.O. 14:00 vs 14:30); Tabelog and Gnavi both show 14:00, treated as authoritative. The Saturday evening session is confirmed. Sunday closure is consistent across all sources. No allergen information is publicly available; all dietary safety assessments are inferred from menu descriptions. The noodle blend ratio (buckwheat-to-wheat) is unconfirmed — calling ahead is essential for any gluten concern.

cuisine: 寿司・江戸前鮨（隠れ家鮨屋）
neighborhood: 中区丸の内
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市中区丸の内2-12-19
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch API; coordinates fall within target box and approximately 1.15km from Nagoya Castle — within 1.61km radius.
seats_text: 11-seat counter only; no tables, no private rooms
cultural_comfort_note: Counter-only omakase in a traditional hidden setting; reservations handled online in Japanese via TableCheck, though booking platforms have some English support.
vegan_detail: Standard Edomae sushi — fish is the centerpiece of every course. No vegan course or substitutions are offered. Dashi is fish-based; tamago (egg) is standard. Not suitable for vegans.
gf_detail: Standard wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu) is used throughout, as is typical in Edomae sushi. The TableCheck reservation page explicitly states the restaurant cannot accommodate allergies arising from shared frying oil or shared cooking utensils. No dedicated GF menu or GF soy sauce alternative is advertised. Celiac diners should contact the restaurant directly before booking.

bio.background: Sushi Uonotana opened on September 16, 2025, as one of two new dining concepts hidden inside 河文 (Kawabun), a historic kaiseki ryotei that has anchored Nagoya's Marunouchi district for over 400 years. Guests once served the Owari Tokugawa clan passed through Kawabun's grand entrance; today, diners slip in through a back service entrance — the 勝手口 — to discover this 11-seat counter entirely separate in spirit from the formal ryotei upstairs. The restaurant is operated by Plan・Do・See, a hospitality group, and occupies renovated back-of-house space within Kawabun's historic building.
bio.origin: 名古屋市中区丸の内 / within 河文 (Kawabun ryotei)
bio.specialty: Edomae omakase sushi — a lunch course at ¥3,800 and dinner at ¥7,800, featuring seasonal nigiri including kombu-jime sea bream, marinated tuna, and salmon roe, finished with red miso soup and tamago
bio.philosophy: Approachable authentic Edomae sushi — the precision of traditional Tokyo-style technique made accessible without the formality or price barrier of a high-end kappo. The kitchen values both craftsmanship and hospitality equally: 握りの技術だけでなく、おもてなしの心も大切にしています (Not only the skill of forming sushi, but also the spirit of hospitality).
roles: female head sushi chef (name not disclosed publicly)
operated by Plan・Do・See hospitality group

anecdotes.text: The entrance has no signage — no noren curtain, no nameplate. Diners walk through the service alley of a 400-year-old imperial ryotei and find an 11-seat counter that feels like a private discovery.

A reviewer who tried the ¥3,800 lunch in late 2025 called it 'top-class value' (コスパ最強クラス), noting that the red-vinegar rice was 'easy for Nagoya people to eat — no harshness' — a subtle nod to the difference between Edo-style shari and local palates.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The TableCheck reservation page explicitly states: 'We cannot accommodate requests if allergic reactions occur due to the use of the same frying oil or cooking utensils, or the manufacturing process.' Shared equipment is confirmed — cross-contamination risk is real.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-containing soy sauce is used throughout the omakase — marinades (漬け maguro), brushed nigiri finishing, and dipping are all standard Edomae practice. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish and seafood are the core of every course. Dashi used in the kitchen (including in the adjacent 勝手口 河内屋) is fish-based. No vegan-safe preparation track exists.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Allergy accommodations with ingredient modifications are available per the TableCheck page, but with explicit caveats: shared frying oil and shared cooking utensils are not manageable. Guests with severe allergies should call 052-211-8322 (10:30–19:00) before booking.

positives.text: Counter format means diners can watch preparation directly and speak with the chef — the best environment to communicate dietary needs in real time.

caveats: Opened September 2025 — a relatively new establishment with a small review base. Hours sourced from Tabelog and TableCheck may differ slightly; Saturday dinner start (16:00 per Tabelog vs 17:30 per some sources) should be confirmed when booking. No official standalone website; reservations and the most current hours are on TableCheck. Chef name not publicly disclosed in any source found.

name_ja: ザ バーキャッスル

neighborhood: 浅間町 (Sengen Cho) / Nishi Ward
vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu. Afternoon tea includes dairy (clotted cream, pastry), eggs, and meat-based savory items.
