gf_detail: The broth is built on tamari soy sauce — even tamari often contains trace wheat in Japanese commercial production. No gluten-free claims or allergen accommodations are mentioned anywhere. The noodles themselves contain wheat. No dedicated fryer information available as this is a noodle shop. Not safe for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The broth is a pork bone and pork meat (豚骨・豚肉) base seasoned with tamari soy sauce. Chashu (braised pork belly) is the signature topping. No vegan or vegetarian options mentioned. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.

bio.background: Opened in October 2018 in Nagoya's Marunouchi business district, Noko Chuka Soba Sato was born from a bold genre pivot: the owner had previously run a popular donburi (rice bowl) restaurant called Le Granbowl at or near the same location, building deep expertise in meat preparation before committing fully to ramen.
bio.specialty: 濃厚チャーシュー中華そば — a rich tamari soy sauce broth loaded with roughly ten thin slices of precisely prepared pork belly arranged in a flower-like spread across the bowl; paper aprons are provided because the broth splashes.
bio.philosophy: The owner insists that despite the dramatic visual of meat-piled bowls, 'the soup itself is the protagonist.' The broth — a concentrated pork-based stock seasoned with tamari and finished with subtle garlic and sweet notes — is treated as the centerpiece, with toppings as supporting cast.

anecdotes.text: The shop transformed from a donburi specialist (Le Granbowl) into a ramen destination, carrying over the owner's confident meat-butchery skill into the chashu preparation that now defines the bowl.
A table condiment of kombu vinegar (昆布酢) is provided to cut the richness of the broth and add umami — an unusual and praised touch that regulars use to shift the flavour mid-bowl.
The shop earned a place on Tabelog's Ramen Aichi 'Tabelog 100' list for 2025, reflecting its standing as one of Nagoya's most respected small ramen counters.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free accommodations exist. Wheat noodles are the core product cooked in the same kitchen, making cross-contamination certain.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broth is seasoned with tamari soy sauce; tamari in Japanese commercial production frequently contains trace wheat, and no wheat-free tamari claim is made.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broth is pork-based; no plant-based or separate cooking line exists.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling protocols or allergen menus are mentioned on any source reviewed. Phone lines are closed during service hours.

caveats: Hours marked irregular: the second Saturday of each month is closed (not just Sundays). Evening service ends when soup runs out, which can be earlier than 20:30. Menu prices sourced from 2024-era blog posts and may have changed. No official website found; Tabelog is the most authoritative source for current status.

address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市西区上名古屋1-8-24
geocode_note: GSI AddressSearch returned coordinates for 愛知県名古屋市西区上名古屋一丁目８番２４号. Distance from Nagoya Castle (35.1856, 136.8997) is approximately 934m — within the 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 名城公園・西区上名古屋（名城公園駅から徒歩約8分）
seats_text: Counter seats only (L-shaped counter, 10 seats)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 10-seat counter shop with a convivial but Japanese-language-only environment; the owner is friendly, but no English signage or menus are evident.
vegan_detail: The broth is tonkotsu (pork bone) and the tare almost certainly contains soy sauce. Chashu pork is a standard topping. No plant-based options are listed. This shop is not suitable for vegans.
gf_detail: No gluten-free claims or accommodations found. The tare (seasoning sauce) in tonkotsu ramen is typically wheat-based soy sauce, making the broth unsafe for celiac diners. Noodles are standard wheat noodles. Gyoza and other sides contain wheat. Cross-contamination risk is high in this tiny open counter kitchen. Not suitable for gluten-free diners.

bio.background: A small, hidden-gem counter ramen shop tucked in a quiet residential street in Nishi Ward, close to Meijo Park. Reviewers describe it as a place that feels more like a neighbourhood izakaya than a ramen shop, with regulars ordering beer and appetisers alongside bowls.
bio.specialty: Tonkotsu ramen with a light, aromatic-oil-enriched pork-bone broth; the house 'Rakuichi-aji' (楽いち味) and 'Chotteri-aji' (ちょってり味 — slightly rich) styles are the calling cards, served with a choice of thin or medium-thick noodles.
bio.philosophy: Unpretentious neighbourhood comfort — the worn exterior belies a welcoming, easy atmosphere where solo diners and locals linger.

anecdotes.text: One Tabelog reviewer wrote that the 'worn exterior does the shop a disservice' — inside, Western music plays and the friendly master makes it feel nothing like a conventional ramen shop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information available. Open counter kitchen in a 10-seat shop makes cross-contamination highly likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tonkotsu tare in Japanese ramen shops standardly contains wheat-based soy sauce; no wheat-free tare is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broth and toppings are pork-based; no plant-based lane exists.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling information found; the shop has no online menu disclosures and is cash-only with no reservation system, making advance allergy communication difficult.

caveats: No official website found. Hours based on Tabelog listing (Tue–Sat only); verify before visiting as small independent shops may change seasonally. Smoking is permitted throughout. No dietary accommodation info exists — GF and vegan ratings are based on standard ramen shop assumptions, not confirmed staff communication.

neighborhood: 浅間町 (Asama-cho), 西区, 名古屋
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市西区新道1丁目2-5
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch API; coordinates fall ~958 m from Nagoya Castle center, within the 1.61 km target radius.
seats_text: 35 seats total: 11 counter seats, table sections, and one semi-private area
cultural_comfort_note: A neighbourhood yakitori bar with a counter atmosphere and Japanese sake focus; English menu or English-speaking staff are not documented, so some Japanese or translation help is advisable.
vegan_detail: A dedicated yakitori specialist built around charcoal-grilled chicken and meat skewers. No plant-based or vegan options are listed on any reviewed source. Hidden animal content (chicken fat, dashi) is likely throughout.
gf_detail: Standard yakitori tare (sauce) almost universally contains wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu, tamari substitution, or allergy accommodation is documented anywhere across Tabelog, HotPepper, or review sources. Some plain salt-grilled (shio) skewers could theoretically be lower-risk, but cross-contamination on shared grills is unaddressed. Celiac diners should call ahead and confirm soy sauce type and grill sharing practices before visiting.

bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled yakitori featuring Nagoya Cochin (名古屋コーチン), a prized heritage chicken breed local to Aichi Prefecture, paired with a carefully curated selection of Japanese sake.

anecdotes.text: Opened November 20, 2024, in the quiet Asama-cho neighbourhood just west of Nagoya Castle. Reviewers noted the quality-to-price ratio feels almost too generous, with one writing it is 'outstanding compared to other yakitori shops' — drawing early praise for chicken heart (hatsu) and tender breast preparations.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Shared charcoal grill used for all skewers; no documentation of separate grill sections or protocols for allergen separation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard yakitori tare typically contains wheat-based soy sauce; no tamari or gluten-free alternative is documented for this restaurant.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All documented menu items are meat-based; animal fat and drippings on a shared grill make vegan cross-contact a near-certainty.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No information found on staff training for allergy handling; no reviews mention dietary accommodation requests being made or fulfilled.

caveats: Opened November 2024; still a relatively new spot with limited review depth. No official website found — all data sourced from Tabelog, HotPepper, and one food blog. GF and vegan ratings reflect absence of any documented accommodation, not a confirmed refusal; a direct phone call (052-446-8133) before visiting is strongly recommended for anyone with dietary restrictions.

name_ja: 中華そば 白壁 あおい
cuisine: Takayama-style shoyu ramen (light soy broth, thin house-made wavy noodles)
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市東区白壁1丁目36
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch to 愛知県名古屋市東区白壁一丁目３６番地. Approximately 1.23 km from Nagoya Castle (35.1856, 136.8997) — within the 1.61 km target radius.
neighborhood: 東大手駅 / 白壁 / 名古屋城北東
seats_text: 8 counter seats only
cultural_comfort_note: Tiny 8-seat counter shop with no English signage or menu; ordering is straightforward but a point at the menu approach is needed for non-Japanese speakers.
vegan_detail: The broth is built on animal stock — chicken carcass, pork bone, and beef — with bonito (katsuobushi) dashi also referenced by reviewers. Signature chashu pork is a standard topping. There are no plant-based options on the menu. Not suitable for vegans.
gf_detail: Wheat is central to this restaurant: house-made thin wavy noodles contain wheat, and the shoyu tare almost certainly contains wheat (standard Japanese soy sauce). No gluten-free noodle alternative or allergen accommodation is offered or mentioned anywhere. The mazesoba and all other menu items similarly contain wheat noodles. Not safe for celiac or wheat allergy.

bio.background: The shop was founded in 1997 by the Sawa family in the Shirakabe district northeast of Nagoya Castle. Ryuichiro Sawa inherited it from his parents and continues to run it today. He is also the founder of the Fujiyama 55 tsukemen chain, but this original family counter is where his roots lie.
bio.specialty: Takayama-style shoyu ramen — a light, clear soy broth made from chicken, pork, and beef stocks with sweet aromatic vegetables, served with thin house-made wavy noodles made fresh each morning
bio.philosophy: Faithfully reproducing the gentle, deeply savory ramen of Hida-Takayama in Gifu Prefecture. The owner made repeated pilgrimages to Takayama to study and perfect the recipe before and after taking over the shop.
bio.origin: Hida-Takayama, Gifu Prefecture (culinary inspiration); shop located in Nagoya's Shirakabe neighbourhood since 1997

anecdotes.text: The current owner visited Takayama multiple times — driven by a taste he could not forget — to study the local ramen style before committing it to his own kitchen. That obsessive field research is reflected in the broth's distinctive sweetness and depth.
Despite dark-coloured shoyu broth, the soup delivers a gentle, not harsh flavour — reviewers consistently note that the appearance is more assertive than the taste, which is soft and umami-rich.
The shop was named a 2025 Tabelog Ramen 100 Best restaurant in Aichi Prefecture, recognising it as one of the region's most respected bowls.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) contains wheat; this restaurant uses shoyu tare in its broth and no tamari or wheat-free alternative is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broth is animal-based (chicken, pork, beef, and bonito); no plant-based cooking zone exists in this tiny 8-seat counter kitchen.

caveats: Phone number confirmed as 052-971-0187 from multiple sources but was listed as unknown on Tabelog itself. The Fujiyama 55 website lists this shop as an affiliated location; however the shop is the original family counter and operates independently in character. Hours confirmed across Tabelog, Hotpepper, and blog sources. No allergen menu or English materials found.

name_ja: オステリア オルモ
cuisine: イタリアン（パスタ・創作イタリア料理）
neighborhood: 那古野（西区）/ 国際センター駅徒歩6分
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市西区那古野1-22-5
geocode_note: GSI AddressSearch returned coordinates [136.891678, 35.17519] for 愛知県名古屋市西区那古野一丁目２２番５号. Approximately 1.37 km from Nagoya Castle (35.1856, 136.8997), within the 1.61 km target radius.
seats_text: カウンター席と2階個室（4〜8名）を含む計15席
hours_raw: 火〜土 18:00〜23:00（LO 21:00）、日・月 定休。不定期で臨時休業・日月営業あり（インスタグラムで告知）。
closed_days: 日曜・月曜（不定期例外あり）
cultural_comfort_note: 完全予約制の高級イタリアン。日本語での事前コミュニケーション（アレルギー等の申告）が必須で、英語対応の確認情報はない。
vegan_detail: 完全なヴィーガンメニューはなし。肉・魚・乳製品を多用するイタリア料理が中心（ウナギ、ホロホロ鳥、ストラッチャテッラチーズなど）。食材の希望は2日前までに伝えれば対応を試みてもらえる可能性がある旨の記載あり（「苦手食材は2日前まで」）。ただし対応範囲はコース変更の都合による。
gf_detail: パスタ（グルテンを含む小麦粉）が料理の中核を占めるイタリアン。セモリナ粉フリット・ブカティーニ・ニョッキなど小麦使用品が多数。醤油・だし等の使用は不明。アレルギーは2日前までの申告で対応を検討してもらえると案内されているが、専用グルテンフリーメニューや交差汚染防止体制の情報はない。セリアックの方は事前に詳細を直接確認要。

bio.background: オーナーシェフの佐藤一也氏は、銀座のAroma Fresca（アロマフレスカ）などで研鑽を積んだのち、2014年11月に名古屋・那古野に当店を開いた。名古屋市西区の「しけ道」（式見道）沿いに建つ築1941年の町家を改装した空間で、木を基調とした温かみある雰囲気が特徴。
bio.origin: 名古屋市西区那古野、2014年11月開業
bio.specialty: 日本の旬食材を用いたイタリア料理。柳橋市場の鮮魚・肉、全国の農家から直接仕入れる自然栽培野菜を「惜しみなく」使い、香りと温度にこだわった一皿を提供する。
bio.philosophy: 「日本の恵みを感じる一皿」を追求しつつ、あくまでイタリア料理の技法・エッセンスを損なわない料理づくりを信条とする。単なる「和イタリアン」に陥らない独自性を意識している。
roles: オーナーシェフ（佐藤一也）

anecdotes.text: 店名「OLMO」はイタリア語で「ケヤキ（欅）」を意味し、那古野の土地に根ざした料理を表現するという意志が込められている。
Gault&Millauから15/20（シェフハット3つ）の評価を受けており、国際的な美食ガイドにも認められた存在。2025年にはTabelog「イタリアンEAST 100選」にも選出。
ヒトサラに寄稿した推薦シェフは「カジュアルながらも非常に繊細で美味しい。関東の有名店で修業したシェフが作り出す季節ごとのメニューと、細部に至る丁寧な仕込みが随所に感じられる」と評している。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 専用グルテンフリー調理器具・フライヤーの分離使用に関する情報は公式サイト・各種レビューとも一切記載なし。パスタ・セモリナ粉・小麦フリットを多用するキッチンでの交差汚染リスクは高いと考えられる。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 醤油・だし（小麦含有の可能性）の使用有無についての情報は公開されていない。イタリア料理が主体だが、日本の旬食材を多用するため隠し味として使用している可能性を排除できない。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 乳製品（ストラッチャテッラ、ゴートチーズ等）・魚介・肉を同一キッチンで扱う。ヴィーガン専用調理ラインの情報なし。

staff_allergy_handling.text: 「アレルギーや苦手食材は2日前までにお知らせいただければ対応します」と案内されている（食べログ情報）。対応の具体的範囲（代替食材の内容・除去の確実性等）は不明。セリアックや重篤なアレルギーの方は予約時に直接詳細を確認することを強く推奨。

positives.text: 完全予約制のため、事前にアレルギー・食材の希望を伝える機会が確保されている。少人数制（15席）で、シェフが一人ひとりの状況に配慮しやすい環境。

caveats: 営業時間は不定期変動あり（インスタグラム確認推奨）。価格帯はコース内容により変動（¥15,000〜¥29,999）。アレルギー対応は2日前までの事前申告制で、具体的な対応範囲は要確認。GF・ヴィーガン対応の確実性は低く、予約時の直接交渉が不可欠。

address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市中区丸の内3-13-17 OIKOS 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 愛知県名古屋市中区丸の内三丁目１３番１７号; approximately 1.16 km from Nagoya Castle (35.1856, 136.8997), within the 1.61 km radius.
neighborhood: 丸の内 / 名古屋城周辺
vegan_detail: The broth is described as 和風出汁 (Japanese-style dashi), which at a standard casual soba shop of this type is virtually always made with katsuobushi (bonito flakes). No vegan-friendly broth option is indicated anywhere. Menu items include meat soba (肉そば). This place is not suitable for vegans.
gf_detail: Traditional soba tsuyu is made with wheat-containing soy sauce (醤油). Tanuki soba — the signature menu item — is topped with tenkasu (tempura batter scraps), which are fried in oil shared with wheat-battered tempura. Buckwheat soba noodles can contain wheat depending on blend (no juwari/100% soba confirmed here). No GF accommodation or awareness indicated at this casual ticket-machine shop. Not appropriate for celiac diners.
seats_text: 立ちカウンター11席、屋外テーブル6卓（2人掛け×5、4人掛け×1）、計25席
cultural_comfort_note: 券売機で食券を購入する完全日本語運用の立ち食いスタイル；英語メニューや英語対応スタッフの存在は確認されていない。

bio.specialty: たぬき蕎麦（天かす入り温かいそば）

anecdotes.text: シンプル イズ ベスト をコンセプトに、丸の内のビジネス街で働くランチ客に、しっかりとした和風出汁の温かいそばを500〜750円台で提供。券売機で食券を購入するスタイルが手軽さを演出している。

gf_cross_contamination.text: たぬき蕎麦のトッピングである天かす（揚げ玉）は小麦粉の天ぷらバッターと同じ油で揚げられており、グルテン交差汚染のリスクが高い。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: そばつゆには通常の醤油（小麦含有）が使用されており、GF対応の代替醤油の情報は確認されていない。

vegan_cross_contact.text: だしは和風出汁（鰹節ベースと推定）であり、植物性ブロスの提供は確認されていない。肉そばメニューも存在する。

caveats: No official website exists; Instagram is the only owned channel. Chef/owner background is entirely undocumented in public sources. Hours confirmed from Tabelog (May 2026 review activity). Dinner service exists Mon-Fri 17:30-20:30 but the blog review focused on lunch — dinner menu may differ slightly. The blog post noted "Monday/Thursday dinner" separately; Tabelog shows Mon-Fri dinner universally — Tabelog taken as authoritative.

address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市東区橦木町1-15 橦木ビル1F
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned 136.913162, 35.180073 for 愛知県名古屋市東区橦木町一丁目１５番地. Distance from target center (Nagoya Castle area 35.1856, 136.8997) is approximately 1.37km, within the 1.61km radius. Within target lat/lng box.
neighborhood: 東大手・東区 (Higashioote, Higashi Ward)
seats_text: Counter seating only, approximately 20 seats. No private rooms. Solo diners and families with children welcome.
hours_raw: Mon–Sat 11:30–14:30, 18:00–23:00; Sun & holidays 11:30–15:00, 17:30–22:00. Closed days listed as irregular (不定休) on Tabelog; one source (nagoya-meshi.com) cited Wednesday closures but this may be outdated.
closed_days: Irregular (不定休); Wednesday closure cited by one source but unconfirmed as current policy
cultural_comfort_note: Counter-only ramen shop with Japanese-language menus displayed above the kitchen; no English signage noted, but solo dining is common and the ordering process is straightforward point-and-pay at the counter.
vegan_detail: All broths are animal-based: chicken clear broth or tonkotsu (pork bone). No plant-based broth options found. Toppings include chicken chashu and pork chashu. Gyoza are also on the menu. No vegan options identified across any source reviewed.
gf_detail: Standard ramen shop using wheat noodles. Soy sauce in the broth is almost certainly wheat-containing (standard Japanese shoyu). No gluten-free noodle alternatives, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodation mentioned in any source. Gyoza wrappers are wheat-based. Not suitable for celiac diners.

bio.background: La・麺喰亭 opened on June 5, 2013 in the quiet Shomokucho neighborhood of Nagoya's Higashi Ward, a short walk from Higashioote Station. It operates as a small independent counter ramen shop with approximately 20 seats.
bio.specialty: 鶏レモンラーメン (Chicken Lemon Ramen, ¥870) — a salt-based clear broth topped with sliced lemon, tender chicken chashu, and white scallion sprouts. The broth is intentionally low-oil, emphasizing clean chicken umami with a bright citrus lift. A second broth option — tonkotsu (pork bone) — is available for those preferring a richer bowl.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen favors a health-conscious, low-oil approach: reviewers consistently note you can finish the soup without heaviness. Lunch sets include complimentary rice and pickles, and affordable add-ons (chashu rice bowls, gyoza) keep the experience accessible.

anecdotes.text: One reviewer described the lemon ramen as 'not just novelty — it was genuinely delicious,' noting that the acidity complements rather than overwhelms the simple broth, making it ramen you'd want to eat endlessly.
The shop is described by regulars as 'a cozy place where you can chat about ramen with the friendly owner,' signaling an engaged, hands-on proprietor running a tight counter operation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese shoyu (wheat-containing soy sauce) is used in ramen broths at this type of shop; no tamari or wheat-free alternative is mentioned in any source.

caveats: Hours discrepancy: Tabelog (primary source) shows dinner until 23:00 Mon–Sat and lists closures as irregular (不定休). An older blog post (nagoya-meshi.com) cited dinner until 20:00 and Wednesday closures — likely outdated. Wednesday is shown as closed in hours_week as a best estimate, but verify before visiting. The hours_week array assumes Wednesday closed based on one source; actual closed days may differ week to week. PayPay QR payment was mentioned on one listing (Retty) while Tabelog says no QR payments — cash-only is the safer assumption.

cuisine: 焼鳥 (omakase yakitori, Mikawa red chicken)
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市西区那古野1-29-13
geocode_note: GSI geocode matched 愛知県名古屋市西区那古野一丁目２９番１３号 exactly. Distance to Nagoya Castle: ~1,456 m, within the 1.61 km radius.
neighborhood: 那古野・四間道 (Nagono / Shikemichi), 国際センター駅2番出口より徒歩3分
seats_text: 12席カウンターのみ
cultural_comfort_note: Reservation-only 12-seat counter experience conducted entirely in Japanese; staff greet guests outside and the intimate omakase format assumes familiarity with the rhythm of a Japanese counter.
vegan_detail: The entire menu is chicken-only omakase. Courses include chicken dashi soup, multiple grilled chicken cuts, and ikura (salmon roe) on the finishing rice dish. No plant-based alternatives exist and there is no indication the kitchen can accommodate vegan requests.
gf_detail: No GF claims found anywhere. Standard yakitori tare (basting sauce) and shoyu-based seasonings almost certainly use wheat-containing soy sauce. The course includes a chicken dashi soup whose thickeners and seasonings are unknown. A yakitori kitchen grilling whole chickens over charcoal has no dedicated fryer risk, but cross-contact from shared utensils and sauces is a real concern. Guests with celiac disease must contact the restaurant directly before booking to ask about soy sauce type and tare ingredients.
caveats: Opened July 2024; still building its track record. Pricing and course structure were confirmed as of late 2024 but may evolve. No allergy or GF information is published anywhere — direct contact is essential for celiac diners.

bio.background: 焼鳥ごりょう opened in July 2024 in a renovated historic townhouse in Nagono (那古野), one of Nagoya's most atmospheric old districts near Shikemichi. It is operated by 合資会社宮治 (Miyaji Partnership), a Nagoya-based hospitality company. The 12-seat counter-only space evokes the intimacy of a high-end sushi bar, and the kitchen works exclusively with whole female Mikawa red chickens (三河赤鶏の雌) — butchering only as many birds as the day's reservations require.
bio.origin: 名古屋市西区那古野・四間道エリア
bio.specialty: Omakase yakitori course built around whole-bird Mikawa red chicken, including rare cuts such as uchi-momo (inner thigh, one per bird), furisode (wing tip), and bonciri (boneless tail), finished with kinoko rice and ikura.
bio.philosophy: Live, waste-nothing whole-bird cookery: every seat is booked before a single chicken is ordered, ensuring absolute freshness. The open counter lets diners watch the entire process from raw bird to finished skewer over charcoal.

anecdotes.text: On Monday and Thursday evenings younger staff take the counter at a discounted ¥5,000 course — a deliberate training programme that regulars use to discover emerging talent.

Reviewers noted that staff come outside to greet arriving guests, and that some visits ended with a small souvenir from the kitchen — touches that give the place a hospitality ethos beyond mere grilling.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on fryer use (no fried items seen in menus), but shared charcoal grills, tare basting sauce, and seasoning bottles are common cross-contact vectors in a yakitori kitchen. No GF protocol is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce type (tamari vs. wheat-containing) is not disclosed anywhere. Standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat; yakitori tare is typically soy-sauce-based. Assume wheat present until confirmed otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is animal-based (chicken, salmon roe, beer/wine). There is no vegan pathway and therefore no relevant cross-contact mitigation to assess.

staff_allergy_handling.text: A Tabelog review notes staff asked 'Is rare chicken (sasami) acceptable?' before serving, suggesting some attentiveness to individual needs, but no formal allergy protocol or allergen menu has been published.

positives.text: No deep-frying observed in the menu; whole-bird charcoal grilling with no batter or breading on skewers is the core technique, which limits some wheat exposure vectors compared to a mixed-menu izakaya.

name_ja: ウッタムカレー 丸の内店
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市中区丸の内3-1-34 堤ビル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch API for 愛知県名古屋市中区丸の内三丁目１番３４号. Coordinates fall within target lat/lng box and 975m from Nagoya Castle — well within 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 中区 丸の内
seats_text: 48 seats across main dining room; private area accommodates small groups up to 8
hours_raw: 11:00-14:30 / 17:00-22:00 daily; no fixed holidays (年中無休)
cultural_comfort_note: Nepalese-run restaurant with Japanese-language menus; staff are friendly though reviewers note some language barrier — pointing at the menu works fine, and service is welcoming to all diners.
vegan_detail: Several plant-based curry choices — vegetable curry, chickpea masala, bean curry, spinach-potato curry, eggplant dry curry — and a vegetable Indian fried rice. However, naan contains dairy (ghee/butter), and there is no stated vegan certification or confirmed dairy-free preparation. Curries appear to be prepared without fish-based dashi or bonito, consistent with South Asian cooking tradition, but no explicit allergen information is published and cross-contact with dairy in the tandoor oven cannot be ruled out. Ask staff to confirm before ordering.
gf_detail: Indian curry sauces at this type of restaurant are typically flour-free, but naan is wheat-based and baked in a shared tandoor oven. Rice is available as a naan substitute. No gluten-free labeling, dedicated procedures, or allergy handling information is published anywhere for this restaurant. Soy sauce is not a typical ingredient in Indian/Nepalese curry, but the kitchen's practices regarding cross-contact are unknown. Anyone with celiac disease should phone ahead before visiting.

bio.background: Uttam Curry opened its Marunouchi branch in December 2013, bringing a compact eatery-style Indian and Nepalese curry shop to the office district just south of Nagoya Castle. It is part of a small multi-location operation — a predecessor Kokusai Center location predates it. Reviews note that staff are Nepalese, and Tabelog's store description states the chef trained in India.
bio.specialty: Large hand-stretched naan baked to order in a tandoor oven, paired with a wide range of curries spanning chicken masala, mutton saag, and several vegetable options; the choice curry lunch set at ¥790 is the calling-card order for the weekday office crowd.
bio.philosophy: Spice levels are kept mild by default to suit Japanese palates, with customization available on request — the stated aim is that everyone from children to adults can enjoy curry.

anecdotes.text: The original Uttam Curry location reportedly sold authentic chicken curry and naan for ¥500 at a community festival in Nishi Ward, which is how one long-time reviewer first discovered the restaurant before it expanded to Marunouchi.
Weekday lunch service is consistently full; a reviewer in 2025 noted the restaurant offers one free extra naan with the lunch set, a touch that has earned repeat visitors.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Naan is wheat-based and baked in a shared tandoor oven used for all breads and tandoori items; no separate GF baking surface or protocol is mentioned anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce is listed in the menu descriptions; Indian and Nepalese curry recipes at this restaurant do not appear to use soy sauce, but no allergen statement is published and kitchen practices are unconfirmed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy (ghee/butter) is used in naan and likely in tandoori dishes; the tandoor oven is shared. Vegan curries are not labeled as prepared separately from dairy-containing items.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy protocol, or dietary accommodation procedure is published. One reviewer noted some language barrier with staff. Phone inquiry (052-202-0177) before visiting is advised for any serious dietary restriction.

positives.text: Multiple vegetable curries are available daily — vegetable curry, chickpea masala, bean curry, spinach-potato, and eggplant dry curry — giving plant-based diners real choices without relying on meat-free adaptations.
Rice is available as a substitute for naan at no stated extra charge, providing a wheat-free carb option if the curry sauce itself is flour-free.

caveats: No official website exists for this restaurant. Hours sourced from Tabelog and Gnavi; no day-by-day variation is documented but hours may differ on public holidays — call ahead. Cash only confirmed via Tabelog payment info. No allergen or GF certification exists; all dietary safety assessments are inferred from menu content and general Indian/Nepalese cooking practice, not confirmed kitchen protocols.

name_ja: あんかけ太郎 名古屋城金シャチ横丁店
name_en: Ankake Taro Nagoya Castle Kinshachi Yokocho
cuisine: あんかけスパ / パスタ
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市中区二の丸1-3 金シャチ横丁宗春ゾーン内
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 愛知県名古屋市中区二の丸１番; approximately 274m from target center — well within 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 名古屋城, 金シャチ横丁宗春ゾーン
seats_text: 33席（テーブル16席、カウンター17席）
closed_days: 12/31〜1/1のみ休業、それ以外年中無休
cultural_comfort_note: A casual counter-and-table pasta spot geared toward local workers and Nagoya Castle tourists — English menus are not confirmed; basic pointing/ordering is manageable but staff likely Japanese-only.
vegan_detail: Ankake spaghetti as served here is not vegan. The signature sauce is built on Worcestershire sauce (which contains anchovies), and the noodles are traditionally coated in lard before saucing. Meat toppings (sausage, bacon, minced meat) are standard across most menu items. A "Japonaise" style listed on the broader menu uses soy-dashi seasoning, adding another non-vegan element. No plant-based alternatives are offered or advertised.
gf_detail: Ankake spaghetti is fundamentally a wheat-pasta dish — gluten is the core ingredient. The sauce also typically contains Worcestershire sauce, which includes wheat-derived vinegar and malt, and bouillon products that may carry gluten. No gluten-free pasta option is offered or mentioned anywhere on the official site or third-party listings. This spot is not suitable for anyone avoiding gluten.

bio.background: Ankake Taro is a Nagoya-born operator dedicated to a single dish: ankake spaghetti, the city's beloved comfort-food oddity in which fat spaghetti noodles are smothered in a thick, spiced tomato-Worcestershire gravy. The brand runs five locations across Naka Ward, and this outpost — the fourth to open — sits inside the Muneharū Zone of Kinshachi Yokocho, the castle-gate dining precinct that opened to revitalize the area around Nagoya Castle's east gate.
bio.origin: 名古屋市中区（名古屋城エリア）
bio.specialty: あんかけスパゲティ（みらかん・ナポリタンなどのトッピング違いを含む）
bio.philosophy: The owner describes the Kinshachi Yokocho location as a responsibility: to be a place that locals who work nearby and tourists alike genuinely need — not just a souvenir stop but a real neighbourhood lunch counter that carries Nagoya's food culture forward.

anecdotes.text: The sauce is deliberately tuned down on spice compared to some rival ankake-spa shops, a deliberate choice to make it accessible to children and older diners — and to maximise the portion-size flexibility that runs from a 250 g 'small' all the way to a 1 kg 'mega'.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire menu is wheat-pasta based and the kitchen is not set up to avoid gluten cross-contact. There is no gluten-free pasta, no GF sauce variant, and no allergen management information published.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The Japonaise menu style uses soy-dashi seasoning; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No wheat-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All cooking stations handle lard-coated noodles and animal-based stocks simultaneously; there is no separation for plant-based preparation.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen chart or staff allergy-handling protocol is published on the official site or any listing reviewed. Callers may reach the restaurant at 052-212-5554 to ask directly.

caveats: Hours sourced from the official store-list page (split lunch/dinner service). The kinshachi-yokocho.com listing shows a longer window (to 22:00 LO 21:00); Tabelog shows 11:00-21:00 flat. Split-service hours from the official site are most likely current but confirmation by phone is advisable. Under30 marked 'no' as this is an established multi-branch operator, not a new entrant.

address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市東区白壁2-26-16
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch API (愛知県名古屋市東区白壁二丁目２６番１６号). Distance from Nagoya Castle (35.1856, 136.8997) is approximately 0.85 km — well within the 1.61 km target radius.
neighborhood: 白壁 (Shirakabe), 東区 (Higashi Ward) — 2-minute walk from Higashiote Station (Meitetsu Seto Line)
seats_text: Counter only, approximately 7–8 seats. No tables, no private rooms.
hours_raw: Seasonal only: open from the autumn equinox (approx. late September) through the spring equinox (approx. late March). Dinner service only; exact nightly hours not published. Closed entirely June–September (off-season). Call 052-961-8222 for current schedule.
closed_days: Closed entirely during summer (spring equinox through autumn equinox, roughly late March–late September). Weekly closed days not published; verify by phone.
cultural_comfort_note: Phone reservation in Japanese required; first-time guests are typically introduced by a regular — this is a local regulars' counter with no English interface or tourist infrastructure.
vegan_detail: Exclusively a fugu (blowfish) speciality restaurant. Every dish centres on pufferfish — sashimi (tessa), hot pot (tetchiri), fried fugu, grilled shirako (milt). There are no plant-based or vegan dishes.
gf_detail: Traditional fugu cuisine relies heavily on wheat-containing soy sauce for ponzu dipping and seasoning. Dashi is fish-based (fugu broth) so dairy-free but not wheat-free by default. No GF adaptations or tamari substitutions are mentioned anywhere in reviews or listings. Fried fugu (kara-age) involves wheat-flour batter. Staff allergy communication should be attempted before booking, but celiac safety cannot be assumed at this type of traditional specialist counter.

bio.background: Nayaten is a tiny, counter-only fugu specialist tucked into the residential Shirakabe neighbourhood of Nagoya's Higashi Ward, a short walk from Higashiote Station and less than a kilometre from Nagoya Castle. It is run by an elderly husband-and-wife couple and operates strictly within the fugu season — from the autumn equinox through to the spring equinox — honouring the traditional view that fugu is at its finest in the cold months.
bio.specialty: Thick-cut fugu sashimi (tessa) — unusually generous slices that concentrate the fish's umami rather than the near-translucent cuts typical elsewhere; also highly regarded for grilled shirako (white milt), tetsu-chiri hot pot, and the proprietors' own deeply infused hire-zake (fin sake).
bio.philosophy: Understated hospitality in a hidden-gem setting: the couple prepare everything themselves, engage guests warmly, and keep the menu tightly seasonal, letting premium fugu speak for itself.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe the counter as a place where you drink and eat happily alongside the chef and proprietress — a rare intimacy at this price point.

First-time visitors cannot simply call to reserve; the convention is to be introduced by an existing regular before a table is offered, making this one of Nagoya's genuinely hard-to-enter neighbourhood counters.
The restaurant is described as extremely difficult to find — a hidden gem tucked in a residential lane — yet beloved by its small circle of loyal regulars.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a traditional Japanese fugu counter. Wheat flour is used in fried fugu preparation (kara-age) and wheat-based soy sauce is standard in ponzu and seasoning throughout the menu. Cross-contamination risk in a tiny 8-seat counter kitchen with no separation of cooking surfaces must be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned in any review or listing. Traditional wheat-containing shoyu is the default for ponzu dipping and cooking.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every component of the menu involves fugu or fish-derived stock. There is no vegan menu path and no plant-based preparation area.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No information on allergy communication or accommodation has been published. Given this is a tiny counter run by two people with no English-language presence, allergy discussions would need to happen in Japanese at the time of reservation.

positives.text: The restaurant is non-smoking throughout (全席禁煙), and fugu itself — as a white fish served as sashimi or in broth — is naturally free of gluten when prepared without soy sauce or flour coating. Guests with specific needs should raise this at booking.

caveats: Currently closed for the summer off-season (open only autumn equinox through spring equinox). Hours and weekly closed days are not published online — call 052-961-8222 to confirm when the season resumes. First-time diners conventionally require an introduction from a regular customer to secure a reservation. No official website exists; all data sourced from Tabelog, Hitosara, and Retty.

address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市中区丸の内2-15-5
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 名古屋市中区丸の内2-15-5 returned [136.898575, 35.174961]. Distance from Nagoya Castle (35.1856, 136.8997) is approximately 1.19 km, within the 1.61 km radius.
neighborhood: 丸の内
seats_text: Counter seats and table seating for about 28 total; counter is well-suited for solo diners.
hours_raw: 火〜土 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30)、17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30)；日曜・祝日定休。月曜については情報によって異なるため要確認。
closed_days: 日曜・祝日（月曜については情報源により異なるため訪問前に要確認）
cultural_comfort_note: A compact neighborhood lunch spot in an office district — no English menu or signage, but the photo menu and simple ordering process make it manageable for non-Japanese speakers.
vegan_detail: The menu is built around seafood tempura — shrimp, scallops, and mixed kakiage — with no plant-based main options. Dashi is almost certainly katsuobushi-based. Nothing here works for vegans.
gf_detail: Tempura is the entire concept: wheat-flour batter is inseparable from every dish. The tsuyu (sauce) and dipping sauces also typically contain wheat-based soy sauce. There is no gluten-free preparation available and no indication the kitchen can accommodate wheat avoidance. Completely unsuitable for celiac diners.

bio.background: Senriki is a small, independently run tempura and donburi shop tucked into the office backstreets of Marunouchi, a five-minute walk from Marunouchi Station. It has built a loyal following among nearby office workers who queue before the doors open at lunch.
bio.specialty: Shio kakiage-don — a generous mixed fritter of small shrimp, mushrooms, and scallops served over rice with a clean salt seasoning rather than the heavy sweet sauce found at most tempura shops.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans into salt as a seasoning philosophy, letting the natural sweetness of the seafood and vegetables speak. Diners can choose salt, tsuyu sauce, or miso — but regulars overwhelmingly recommend salt.

anecdotes.text: A blogger arrived before noon on a weekday to find a queue already formed. A fellow diner in line said unprompted: 'Rather than trying three varieties, go with one specialty — the salt kakiage is what this place is about.'

The restaurant offers a 'kurabehare' (comparison) set at around ¥1,100–¥1,300 that lets diners order two mini bowls side by side — a practical way to sample the salt versus miso preparations in one sitting.
A daily 'kigamayure-don' (whim bowl) rotates based on what the kitchen feels like — past examples have included Hida beef with tomato and crab with smoked salmon and yuzu pepper.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat flour batter is used for every dish — there is no separation of gluten-containing and gluten-free cooking at any stage.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tsuyu-based dipping sauce and seasoning sauces are standard Japanese preparations and almost certainly contain wheat-based soy sauce; no tamari or GF alternative is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All mains contain seafood (shrimp, scallops); dashi is presumed katsuobushi-based with no plant-based alternative mentioned.

caveats: Monday hours are inconsistent across sources — Tabelog lists Monday as closed while blog sources indicate Mon–Sat lunch and dinner service. Call ahead (052-204-1118) to confirm current Monday status before visiting. Hours may also shift seasonally or around holidays.

address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市西区樋の口町3-19 エスパシオ ナゴヤキャッスル 4F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch for 愛知県名古屋市西区樋の口町３番１９号; coordinates confirmed within target box and approximately 330m from Nagoya Castle center — well within 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 西区 樋の口町 / 浅間町駅から徒歩約10分
vegan_detail: Traditional kaiseki menus rely heavily on dashi (typically katsuobushi/bonito-based) and seafood courses. No vegan menu or plant-based alternative is advertised. The counter-style omakase format ("御誂え料理") allows some customization if discussed at time of reservation, but vegan accommodation is not confirmed and would require direct inquiry before booking.
gf_detail: Standard kaiseki uses wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu) in sauces and marinades, and dashi may include wheat-containing ingredients. No gluten-free menu is offered. The restaurant's policy states allergen requests must be made at the time of booking — no post-arrival adjustments are guaranteed. There is no dedicated gluten-free program, no dedicated fryer disclosed, and no evidence of cross-contamination protocols. Celiac diners should contact the restaurant directly before reserving.
hours_raw: Tue–Sun 11:30–14:30, 18:00–22:00; closed Monday (or Tuesday if Monday is a public holiday)
closed_days: 月曜日定休（月曜日が祝日の場合は火曜日）
seats_text: 46 seats total, including counter seating and two private rooms accommodating up to 6 guests each. All seatings are simultaneous — guests are asked to arrive 5 minutes early.
cultural_comfort_note: A formal hotel kaiseki restaurant operating on simultaneous fixed seatings with a smart-casual dress code — English is unlikely to be spoken fluently at the counter, and interaction with the head chef is in Japanese.
caveats: Opened October 1, 2025; only 18 Tabelog reviews as of research date, so ratings and service quality impressions may still be settling. A 15% service charge applies. The Gion Sasaki supervisor Hiroshi Sasaki hosts occasional special one-night events (e.g., July 2026 at ¥40,000/person) that differ from the standard menu.

bio.background: Nagata Isao was born in 1972 in Gifu Prefecture and entered the culinary world at age 18, spending his formative years at the original Hotel Nagoya Castle and its sister property Castle Plaza. He became head chef at Castle Plaza in 2009, then head of Japanese cuisine at Hotel Nagoya Castle in 2013, and later served as Japanese cuisine chef at Nagoya Kanko Hotel's 'Gorotake' from 2020. When Espacio Nagoya Castle opened in October 2025 — built on the site of the former Hotel Nagoya Castle — he returned as head chef of 丈. He received the Aichi Prefectural Governor's Excellence Technician Award (愛知県知事優秀技能者表彰) in 2022, one of the prefecture's highest culinary honors.
bio.specialty: Counter-style kaiseki (カウンター割烹) featuring seasonal omakase courses — 御誂え料理 — timed and paced to each diner, with ingredients sourced nationwide for peak seasonal quality.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant operates under the motto 温古知新 — 'learn from the past to create the new' — a guiding principle of its supervising chef Hiroshi Sasaki of Gion Sasaki in Kyoto. The name 丈 (jo) is chosen deliberately: it carries the meaning of integrity and uprightness, and shares the same reading as 城 (castle), linking the restaurant's identity to Nagoya Castle directly outside its windows.
bio.origin: Opened October 1, 2025 as the flagship Japanese restaurant of Espacio Nagoya Castle, a new luxury hotel erected on the historic site of Hotel Nagoya Castle. The hotel's Japanese cuisine lineage stretches back decades, and 丈 is designed as its direct heir — with the same chef who cooked for dignitaries during the 2005 Aichi Expo now leading the kitchen.
roles: Supervising Producer: 佐々木浩 (Sasaki Hiroshi) — proprietor of Gion Sasaki, Kyoto (3 Michelin stars)

anecdotes.text: Nagata Isao catered to foreign dignitaries and VIP guests during the 2005 Aichi Expo while working at Hotel Nagoya Castle — a formative test of high-stakes hospitality that now informs his exacting standards at 丈.
The name 丈 shares its reading (じょう / jo) with 城 (castle), quietly embedding Nagoya Castle into the restaurant's very identity. Reviewers have noted the wordplay as intentional and considered.

Hiroshi Sasaki — the three-Michelin-star chef who oversees monthly menus at 丈 from his Kyoto base — is scheduled to personally cook a one-night special event at the restaurant in July 2026 for ¥40,000 per guest, offering diners a direct encounter with his technique.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free preparation area or cross-contamination protocols are disclosed. A kaiseki kitchen uses wheat-based shoyu and mirin routinely across multiple dishes; shared prep surfaces and equipment are the norm in this format.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese kaiseki relies on shoyu (soy sauce brewed with wheat) as a foundational seasoning in sauces, marinades, and finishing. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere in available sources.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi (stock) in kaiseki is typically made with katsuobushi (bonito flakes) or niboshi (dried sardines) and is used throughout the menu — in soups, sauces, and seasonings. No plant-based dashi option is advertised.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official site states that allergy and dietary concerns must be communicated at the time of reservation. No adjustments are guaranteed after arrival. Guests with serious allergies should call 052-908-8930 (10:00–18:30) before booking.

positives.text: The counter omakase format ('御誂え料理') allows the kitchen to pace and adjust individual courses in real time, which in principle creates more opportunity for dietary discussion than a fixed set menu — though this is subject to advance notice at booking.

address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市西区樋の口町3-19 エスパシオ ナゴヤキャッスル 4F
geocode_note: GSI geocode matched to 愛知県名古屋市西区樋の口町3番19号; coordinates place the building ~330m southwest of Nagoya Castle, well within 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 西区 (Nishi Ward) / 浅間町・名古屋城エリア
seats_text: 60 seats total; 5 private rooms seating 6, 6, 8, 8, and 12 guests respectively; table seating also available.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–14:30 (L.O. 13:30) / Dinner 17:30–22:00 (L.O. 20:30); open daily, no regular closure days listed
closed_days: No regular closure days listed; hours subject to change — verify before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: Luxury hotel restaurant (Espacio Nagoya Castle) with international guest infrastructure; English-language reservation platforms (TableCheck, Ikyu) available and hotel staff accustomed to overseas visitors.
vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu exists. Cantonese cuisine here centers on live-tank seafood, dim sum (typically contain lard or egg wrappers), Nagoya cochin chicken, and braised pork belly. Hidden animal ingredients (oyster sauce, lard in pastry, dashi-equivalent stocks) are standard in this style. Vegan requests should be made well in advance; the kitchen may be able to accommodate with prior notice but this is not confirmed.
gf_detail: Standard Cantonese kitchen with significant wheat exposure: soy sauce (shoyu) is used throughout savory dishes, dim sum wrappers contain wheat flour, and cornstarch-thickened sauces may be cross-contaminated. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or GF soy sauce alternative is documented. The hotel notes allergy accommodations should be requested in advance and may be declined if safety cannot be guaranteed. Not safe for celiac without direct, detailed inquiry with the kitchen.

bio.background: 柳城 NAGOYA CASTLE opened on October 1, 2025, as the signature Chinese restaurant of Espacio Nagoya Castle, a luxury hotel that occupies a historic site roughly 330 meters from Nagoya Castle's walls. The restaurant carries on the 柳城 (Ryujo) brand, referencing the castle's old name.
bio.specialty: Cantonese crispy pork (広東式クリスピーポーク) and hand-crafted dim sum by a dedicated master dim sum chef; live-tank seafood dishes prepared to order.
bio.philosophy: Head chef Katsuhiro Naito draws on traditional Cantonese technique while weaving in the umami of local Japanese ingredients — Nagoya cochin, Okazaki ouhan chicken — to create what he calls authentic Cantonese cuisine expressed through a Japanese ingredient lens. Beverage pairings (Chinese wines, Taiwanese teas) are curated by in-house sommeliers and tea masters.
roles: Head Chef: 内藤勝博 (Katsuhiro Naito)

anecdotes.text: Chef Naito is the first Japanese chef to reach the top 10 in an international Cantonese cuisine competition, a distinction cited prominently in the hotel's opening press materials.

The restaurant overlooks Nagoya Castle and takes its name from the castle's historical designation 柳城 (Ryujo — Willow Castle), connecting the dining experience to the 400-year-old landmark visible through its windows.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information about cross-contamination controls is publicly documented. Standard Cantonese kitchen workflows involve shared woks, utensils, and prep surfaces with wheat-containing ingredients throughout service.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Wheat-containing soy sauce is a foundational ingredient in Cantonese cooking and used throughout the menu. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Lard is traditional in dim sum pastry, and oyster sauce and other animal-derived condiments are ubiquitous in Cantonese kitchens. No vegan cross-contact protocols are documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The hotel states that allergy accommodations require advance notice and may be declined if customer safety cannot be guaranteed; requests should be directed to the reservation desk (052-908-8930, 10:00–18:30).

positives.text: Luxury hotel setting with dedicated reservation staff, making advance allergy communication more feasible than at a typical independent restaurant.

caveats: Opened October 2025; hours and closure days may change — confirm before visiting. No dietary-specific menu published; all GF/vegan accommodation requires direct advance inquiry with the kitchen. Service charge of 15% applies. Phone for reservations: 052-908-8930 (10:00–18:30).

cuisine: 居酒屋・おばんざい・和食
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市中区丸の内2-17-6 丸の内ホリエビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode matched to 愛知県名古屋市中区丸の内二丁目１７番６号; 1.3km from Nagoya Castle, within 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 中区丸の内（丸の内駅より徒歩2分）
seats_text: 13席（カウンター席メイン、個室4名まで）
cultural_comfort_note: Small counter-style izakaya run by a solo female proprietor; no English signage or menus reported, suited to Japanese-comfortable diners.
vegan_detail: Traditional obanzai cooking built on dashi broth; reviewers cite fish-based dashi (鰹・魚介出汁) as central to the flavour. Dishes like braised pork (角煮) and mackerel (ほっけ) appear on the menu alongside vegetable small plates. No vegan options are advertised, but plant-forward dishes (tofu with greens, seasonal vegetables) do appear — ask staff what is prepared without dashi or animal protein. Hidden fish-based dashi is the main concern.
gf_detail: No gluten-free labelling or accommodation is mentioned anywhere. Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is almost certainly used in dashi-maki tamago and braised dishes. Tempura-battered items may appear. Cross-contamination risk is real in a 13-seat kitchen with no dedicated GF protocol. Celiac diners should not assume safety; ask specifically about soy sauce and cooking oils before ordering.

bio.background: A small obanzai counter that opened in September 2025 in the Horie Building on Marunouchi-dori, a short walk from Marunouchi Station. Run by a female proprietor, the place seats just 13 at a counter and a compact private nook.
bio.specialty: Dashi-forward obanzai small plates — seasonal vegetable simmers, dashi egg roll (dashimaki tamago), and braised dishes paired with sake and shochu.
bio.philosophy: Gentle, home-style flavours rooted in careful dashi technique; a welcoming spot for solo diners seeking a quiet neighbourhood counter.

anecdotes.text: One early reviewer noted that, despite the name conjuring an image of an afro-wearing cook, the establishment is run by a warm female proprietor.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information available. The kitchen serves a full izakaya menu in a compact space; cross-contamination cannot be ruled out.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-containing soy sauce is expected in braised dishes (kakuni), egg roll, and simmered vegetable plates. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi (fish stock) is central to the cooking style per multiple reviews; plant-based dishes are likely prepared in the same pots and with the same dashi as fish dishes.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling policy or staff training information has been reported. The restaurant is a solo-operator counter; direct verbal communication with the proprietor is the only available channel.

caveats: No official website or social media presence found for this restaurant. All data sourced from Tabelog (listing + reviews). The restaurant opened September 2025 and has limited review coverage (13 reviews at time of research). Smoking is permitted according to at least one reviewer, which conflicts with the non-smoking flag on the listing — verify directly. No dietary accommodation information is available; all GF/vegan flags are assessments based on typical izakaya/obanzai cooking practices.

name_ja: ラ・グランターブル ドゥ キタムラ
neighborhood: 高岳駅周辺（東区主税町）
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市東区主税町4-84
geocode_note: GSI geocode confirmed for 東区主税町四丁目84番地 → [136.917587, 35.180908]. Calculated distance from Nagoya Castle (35.1856, 136.8997) is approximately 1.71 km — marginally outside the 1.61 km threshold by ~100 m. Restaurant falls within the Tabelog sub-area 高岳/東区 as listed, confirming it is in the intended coverage zone.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu exists. This is classic French fine dining with heavy use of butter, cream, meat, and seafood. The kitchen customizes menus at reservation time and asks about dislikes and allergies, so vegan-adjacent adjustments may be possible for some courses, but this is not a vegan-friendly restaurant. Inquire directly when booking.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or certification. French cuisine at this level routinely uses wheat-based sauces, flour-thickened preparations, and bread service. The kitchen solicits allergy and dietary restrictions at reservation, suggesting willingness to adapt, but cross-contamination controls and soy-sauce/wheat practices are unknown. Celiacs should call ahead and discuss in detail before booking.
seats_text: 60席（個室6〜10名含む）
cultural_comfort_note: Upscale Japanese French fine dining; staff are professional and accustomed to accommodating preferences discussed at reservation, but English-language service is not advertised and non-Japanese speakers should not assume it.

bio.background: Born in 1964 in Gifu Prefecture, Kitamura entered the culinary world at the Gifu Kaikan. He left for France at age 23, training at three-Michelin-star establishments Charles Barrier (widely regarded as a father of nouvelle cuisine), Jamain, and Taillevent — some of the most prestigious houses in Paris. He then served as sous-chef at Girardet in Switzerland, at the time considered one of the world's great restaurants. Returning to Japan, he spent three years as head chef at Mikuni Nagoya, then in 2004 opened his own house in Nagoya's historic Shirakabe white-walled district.
bio.specialty: A personalized, never-repeated tasting menu built around that day's finest ingredients — signature preparations have included Canadian lobster shaped into a heart with pumpkin mousse and wagyu filet with natural abalone surrounded by seasonal vegetables.
bio.philosophy: Kitamura operates by two interlocking principles: 'Cuisine Spontanée' — natural cooking inherited from Girardet — and 'a restaurant is a theater,' staging the entire experience through 7-meter ceilings, tableside plating, and rolling dessert and cheese wagons. He also holds that no returning guest should ever receive the same dish twice, keeping menus on record and improvising entirely from the day's best market finds. He has said: 'Like haute couture clothing, cuisine should also be haute couture.'
bio.origin: 名古屋市東区（旧白壁武家屋敷エリア）

anecdotes.text: The restaurant occupies a converted Meiji-era residence in Nagoya's Shirakabe district — a neighborhood once home to samurai estates and later to the city's most powerful industrialists. The interior pairs 7-meter ceilings and classical European appointments with the bones of a traditional Japanese townhouse.
Kitamura insists on using every part of every ingredient as a gesture of respect to the farmers and fishermen who produce them, making zero-waste cooking a kitchen-wide ethic rather than a trend.
Tabelog recognized the restaurant in its French EAST Top 100 in 2021, 2023, and 2025, placing it among the most consistently regarded French tables in eastern Japan.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on cross-contamination controls is publicly available. French tasting-menu kitchens routinely handle wheat flour for sauces, bread, and pastry throughout service, creating elevated cross-contact risk for celiacs.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce usage confirmed or denied. Classic French cuisine at this level typically does not rely on soy sauce, but certain Japanese-French fusion preparations may include it. Status unknown — ask at reservation.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Butter, cream, and animal stocks are core to French cuisine at this level. No plant-based preparation stations or protocols are mentioned.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The kitchen requests that guests disclose allergies and disliked ingredients when making a reservation. Menus are customized accordingly, suggesting a willingness to accommodate, though the scope of modifications for serious allergens like gluten is unconfirmed.

positives.text: Menu customization at reservation is standard practice here — the kitchen maintains guest records and never repeats dishes for returning diners, indicating genuine flexibility in composition.

caveats: Closed days are irregular — always confirm before visiting. The geocoded address (東区主税町4-84) places the restaurant approximately 1.71 km from Nagoya Castle, which is marginally outside the stated 1.61 km radius; it is nonetheless within the correct Tabelog sub-area (高岳/東区). Dietary accommodation details (gluten-free scope, vegan substitutions) are not publicly documented — contact the restaurant directly at 052-933-3900 when booking. All hours should be reconfirmed as the restaurant may close on unannounced dates.

name_ja: 大銀杏 名古屋西店
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市西区城西2-22-5 グランレガリア1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI AddressSearch for 西区城西2-22-5. Approximately 800m from Nagoya Castle (35.1856, 136.8997), well within the 1.61km target radius. Note: the brief listed address 西区上名古屋1-7-10 does not match this restaurant's actual address; and the Tabelog URL in the brief (23004088) resolves to an unrelated closed bakery. The correct Tabelog ID for 大銀杏 名古屋西店 is 23066057.
neighborhood: Josai, Nishi-ku — 3-min walk from Asama-cho Station (Tsurumai Line)
seats_text: 46 seats across counter and table seating
hours_raw: 17:30〜22:30（LO 22:00）; 定休日：木曜日（不定休の記載もあり）
closed_days: Thursdays (irregular closures also noted on official site — confirm before visiting)
cultural_comfort_note: Neighborhood izakaya atmosphere near a residential area of Nishi-ku; Japanese menus and counter seating; basic Japanese useful for ordering at the charcoal counter.
vegan_detail: This is a dedicated yakitori restaurant — the entire menu revolves around grilled chicken and offal. The house seasoning salt is made from bonito (katsuobushi) and kombu dashi, making even the salt-grilled items non-vegan. No plant-based skewer alternatives are offered. Vegetarians and vegans have essentially no options here beyond side salads or cold tofu if available, but these are not confirmed allergen-free.
gf_detail: Standard yakitori with no gluten-free declaration. Tare (dipping sauce) used on many skewers contains wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu). The house salt blend is made from bonito and kombu dashi and is advertised as additive-free, but wheat-free status is unconfirmed. Charcoal grilling reduces cross-contamination risk versus frying, but shared grills and shared prep areas are standard. No dedicated GF menu or allergen guide found on any source. Celiacs should ask about tare vs. shio (salt) options and confirm shoyu ingredients before ordering.

bio.background: Ooicho is a Nagoya-based charcoal yakitori chain with locations in Sakae, Meieki, and this Nishi-ku outpost near Asama-cho Station. No founding date or owner biography is publicly documented.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled chicken skewers using Aichi Prefecture's Nishiki-sawayaka chicken, finished with a housemade salt blended from bonito and kombu dashi — no chemical additives.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen prioritizes sourcing quality local chicken and grilling over high-heat binchotan charcoal on a custom extra-large grill to seal in natural umami, letting the ingredient speak rather than heavy seasoning.

anecdotes.text: The Nagoya Nishi branch was selected as a Tabelog Yakitori Top 100 restaurant, notable for a neighborhood spot rather than a city-center flagship.
Rare cuts such as haatsu moto (chicken heart base, requiring 5-6 birds per single skewer) reflect the kitchen's commitment to whole-bird butchery.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated GF kitchen or allergen separation is documented. Shared charcoal grill used for all skewers including tare-marinated items.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tare (sweet soy-based glaze) used on standard yakitori skewers contains wheat. Shio (salt-grilled) option uses housemade salt from bonito and kombu dashi; wheat-free status of this salt is unconfirmed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All skewers are animal-based and the grill is entirely shared. Bonito-kombu salt is used across the menu. No vegan-safe items identified.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu or allergy handling policy is listed on any available source. Phone inquiry (052-325-5626) before visiting is strongly advised for any dietary restriction.

positives.text: Charcoal grilling rather than deep-frying eliminates shared-fryer cross-contamination risk. Shio (salt-only) skewers avoid tare sauce for those avoiding soy; ask staff to confirm which skewers can be ordered shio.

caveats: IMPORTANT ADDRESS/URL DISCREPANCY: The brief's Tabelog URL (23004088) resolves to an unrelated closed bakery (ブーランジェリー パリパリ) in Meitouku. The brief's address 西区上名古屋1-7-10 does not match 大銀杏 名古屋西店's confirmed address of 西区城西2-22-5. The record here is for the confirmed 大銀杏 名古屋西店 at 城西2-22-5, Tabelog ID 23066057. If the intended target was a different restaurant at 上名古屋1-7-10, that establishment could not be confirmed. Hours show some inconsistency across sources (LO 22:00 per official site vs. LO 22:30 per search aggregators); call ahead to confirm. Closed days listed as Thursday but official site also says 不定休 (irregular).

neighborhood: 東区 東大手 (Higashi Ward, Higashi-Ote)
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市東区東外堀町11
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 愛知県名古屋市東区東外堀町11番地. Approx 990m from Nagoya Castle — within the 1.61km target radius.
vegan_detail: Entirely eel-focused menu. All dishes built around unagi; soups are fish-based (鯉 or dashi); no plant-based options offered. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
gf_detail: Traditional unagi tare (kabayaki glaze) is made with standard wheat-containing soy sauce — a significant gluten source. Dashi used in soups is fish-based but likely made with standard ingredients. No GF claims on the menu or website. The restaurant does not advertise any allergen accommodations. Guests with celiac disease should call ahead (052-951-8781) and ask specifically about the soy sauce and tare; substitution is unlikely at a traditional specialist of this type. Cross-contamination risk from shared kitchen surfaces must also be assumed.
hours_raw: 火～土 11:00〜13:30（昼のみ）。土曜夜は完全予約制で営業あり（17:30頃）。日・月・祝日・土用の丑の日は定休。
closed_days: 日曜、月曜、祝日、土用の丑の日
seats_text: 82席。1階にテーブル席、個室（6〜8名）あり。畳の間も備える。
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, traditional unagi specialist with Japanese-only signage and menus; staff interaction is polite but not English-oriented — pointing to menu items works fine for ordering.

bio.background: Founded in the late Edo period, Unagi Kiya is one of Nagoya's oldest surviving eel restaurants, now run by its fifth generation of ownership. It sits a short walk from Higashi-Ote Station in the historically layered Higashi Ward, not far from Nagoya Castle.
bio.specialty: Unagi don (eel rice bowl) and ohitsu-mabushi — the house style features Kansai-influenced belly-split eel grilled directly over binchotan charcoal without steaming, producing a crisper exterior than Edo-style preparations. The thick-cut eel is sourced from Kagoshima, stored live in well water before service, and finished with a century-old continuing tare.
bio.philosophy: Simplicity and continuity: the same tare has been topped off and carried forward across generations, the well water is still used to rest live eel before grilling, and the menu has stayed deliberately short.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant keeps live eel in well water before preparation — a practice said to reduce stress in the fish and improve texture and flavor at service.
Signed celebrity memorabilia is displayed inside, evidence of a long-standing following beyond the neighborhood regulars.
Liver dishes (kimo-yaki, umaki) are notably absent from the menu — a deliberate choice that sets Kiya apart from most unagi specialists in the city.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Traditional unagi tare (kabayaki glaze) is made with standard wheat-containing soy sauce. No GF alternative is offered or advertised. Cross-contamination from shared grilling surfaces and kitchen equipment must be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature tare — built up over generations from standard Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat) — is central to every eel dish on the menu. There is no wheat-free soy sauce option indicated anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is eel-based; fish dashi is used in all soups. No plant-based cooking surfaces or utensils are separated. Not applicable for vegan diners.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling policy is published on the official site or Tabelog. Reservations are phone-only; calling ahead at 052-951-8781 is the only way to discuss dietary needs before visiting.

positives.text: Eel is prepared fresh to order (approximately 20–40 minutes), meaning there is no pre-made batch sitting with mixed ingredients — though the tare itself remains a fixed wheat source.

caveats: Hours marked irregular: Saturday dinner service (reservation-only, ~17:30) is reported by multiple sources but not confirmed on the official site. Confirm current Saturday evening availability by phone. Monday closure reportedly added from April 2025 — verify before visiting. The restaurant closes on Doyo no Ushi no Hi (Day of the Ox), which falls twice in July–August; check the date annually.

cuisine: カフェ・ビストロ（無農薬野菜中心）
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市中区丸の内1-12-3
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 愛知県名古屋市中区丸の内1-12-3 returned [136.893661, 35.175858]. Distance from Nagoya Castle (35.1856, 136.8997) is approximately 1.21 km, within the 1.61 km radius.
neighborhood: 中区丸の内（堀川沿い、丸の内駅8番出口より徒歩5分）
seats_text: 24席（カウンター6席、テーブル18席）。イベント時は着席20名・立食30名まで対応可。
closed_days: 月曜定休、毎月第1火曜日休み（不定休あり）
cultural_comfort_note: メニューや案内は日本語中心だが、カード決済対応・予約可能でアクセスしやすい環境。英語対応の明示はなし。
vegan_detail: 野菜中心のメニューが多いが、キッシュ（卵・クリーム使用）、フォカッチャ、シフォンケーキなど乳製品・卵を含む料理が主力。ランチコースには豚肉（知多産）や魚介も登場する。完全ヴィーガン対応の明示はなく、隠しだし（鰹・煮干し）使用の有無も不明。事前に電話で確認することを強く推奨。
gf_detail: 小麦を含むフォカッチャ・キッシュ生地・パスタが主要メニュー。グルテンフリー対応の明示なし。醤油使用の有無不明。専用フライヤーや交差汚染対策の記載もなし。セリアック病対応は期待できず、事前確認必須。

bio.background: 明治40年（1907年）創業の材木商・沖正商店が営む老舗の蔵をリノベーションして誕生したギャラリーカフェ。堀川沿いに位置し、高い天井と木のぬくもりが特徴的な空間で、ギャラリーとカフェが一体となった稀有な場所。3つのギャラリースペースでは現代アートの展覧会、ポップアップ、朗読会などが開催される。
bio.origin: 愛知県名古屋市中区丸の内（堀川沿い）
bio.specialty: キッシュランチ（1日5食限定）／無農薬野菜たっぷりのサラダ
bio.philosophy: アートを身近に感じられる場所をつくりたいという想いから生まれた空間。高知県嶺北地域の野菜や愛知県産の地場野菜を中心に、身体にやさしい素材を大切にしたビストロ料理を提供。亜硫酸塩添加量を抑えたナチュラルワインも揃える。

anecdotes.text: 2019年「愛知サラダメシ総選挙」でブロンズ賞を受賞。無農薬野菜を活かした彩り豊かなサラダが評価された。
明治40年創業の材木商が営む蔵をリノベーション。天井の高い開放的な空間に、ギャラリーとカフェが共存する。ギャラリーは独立した設計で、アート鑑賞だけを目的とした来訪者も食事客も快適に過ごせる。

gf_cross_contamination.text: グルテンフリー対応・交差汚染対策についての記載は公式サイト・各グルメサイトのいずれにも確認できなかった。フォカッチャやパスタなど小麦料理が主力のため、厨房内での小麦粉との接触は避けられない可能性が高い。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 醤油の使用有無については公開情報に記載なし。フレンチ・ビストロ系メニューのため頻繁な使用は考えにくいが、確認は取れていない。

vegan_cross_contact.text: キッシュ・フォカッチャ・シフォンケーキなど乳製品・卵を含む料理が中心。完全ヴィーガンへの交差接触対策についての記載なし。

staff_allergy_handling.text: Tabelogの情報として「予算やメニューに関するご要望にも対応可能」との記載があり、事前相談に応じる姿勢は示されているが、アレルギー対応の専門的なプロトコルの有無は不明。来店前の電話確認（052-265-5557）を強く推奨。

positives.text: 無農薬野菜（高知県嶺北地域産・愛知県産）を積極使用。野菜中心のメニュー構成で、植物性素材の選択肢は他の一般的なカフェより多い。
2019年「愛知サラダメシ総選挙」ブロンズ賞受賞。野菜料理へのこだわりが第三者機関によって評価されている。

caveats: 第1火曜日が定休の可能性あり（情報源によって記載が異なる）。ランチはカード不可との情報もあり、現金持参を推奨。公式サイト（blanka.co.jp）はSSL証明書の問題でアクセス不可のため、最新情報は電話またはInstagram（@cafe_blanka）で要確認。

cuisine: あんかけスパゲッティ (Ankake Spaghetti / Nagoya-style thick-sauce pasta)
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市西区上名古屋4-3-12
geocode_note: GSI AddressSearch returned [136.896133, 35.194729] for 愛知県名古屋市西区上名古屋4-3-12. Coordinates fall within the target bounding box and approximately 1,064 m from Nagoya Castle — within the 1.61 km radius.
neighborhood: 西区 上名古屋 / 浄心駅から徒歩10分
seats_text: 9席（カウンター7席、2人掛けテーブル1卓）
hours_raw: 火〜水・土〜日 11:30〜15:00（L.O.14:30）、木のみ夜18:00〜20:00（L.O.19:30）も営業、月・金定休。臨時休業あり（Instagramで確認要）。
closed_days: 月曜・金曜（臨時休業あり）
cultural_comfort_note: 小さなカウンター中心の店で英語メニューや英語対応の記載はなく、日本語での注文が基本となる。
vegan_detail: メニューの看板はフィッシュプレートなど魚介系メニューで、ソースは野菜ベースのマイルドな旨味とされているが、ビーガン対応の明示はない。肉・魚を含まない選択肢があるかは不明のため、来店前に確認が必要。
gf_detail: あんかけスパゲッティは小麦粉の麺が主体であり、グルテンフリー対応の記載は一切ない。セリアック病・小麦アレルギーの方には非対応とみなすべき。

bio.background: もともとスパイスカレーの「LIT COFFEE」として出発し、その後約3年間キッチンカーであんかけパスタを全国のイベントで提供してきた。2026年1月12日、名古屋市西区上名古屋の路地に実店舗をオープン。店のロゴに馬をあしらったのは「自由に動き回るイメージ」から。年明けに合わせた開業は「午年のタイミング」とも重なった。
bio.specialty: あんかけスパゲッティ（S/M/L・20種以上のトッピングから選択可）。看板はフィッシュプレート：揚げた白身魚に野菜の甘みを引き出したマイルドなあんかけソースをかけたもので、塩気と甘みのバランスが特徴とされる。
bio.origin: 名古屋市西区上名古屋
bio.philosophy: キッチンカー時代の熱量をそのまま実店舗に持ち込む、移動する食の精神。レトロ喫茶の雰囲気の中で、名古屋めしのあんかけパスタを真剣に掘り下げる。
roles: オーナーシェフ（名前非公開）

anecdotes.text: キッチンカー時代から看板だったフィッシュプレートは「旨みがぎゅっと詰まった」一皿として、実店舗オープン直後から話題を集め、営業時間中に売り切れることも珍しくない。
店のロゴはキッチンカー時代から使い続けている馬のモチーフ。「自由に動き回る」イメージを体現し、2026年の寅年（午年）開業とのタイミングも「素敵な一致」と地元メディアが紹介。

caveats: 営業時間・臨時休業はInstagramで頻繁に更新されるため、来店前に必ず確認のこと。木曜のディナー営業（18:00〜20:00）は公式Tabelog情報に基づくが変更の可能性あり。席数9のみのため売り切れ閉店の可能性が高い。グルテン・アレルギー情報は公式に開示されておらず、本記録は「非対応」と判断した。

cuisine: 日本料理・割烹（なにわ料理）
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市東区泉1-14-23 ホワイトメイツ 3F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned exact match for 東区泉一丁目１４番２３号. Distance to Nagoya Castle (35.1856, 136.8997) is approximately 1,400m — within the 1.61km radius.
neighborhood: 東区泉・久屋大通
seats_text: カウンター席と個室（4〜8名）を備え、合計18席（ヒトサラは26席と記載するが、Tabelog記載の18席を採用）
hours_raw: 月〜土 17:30〜22:00、日曜・祝日定休。ランチ営業なし。
cultural_comfort_note: Upscale counter-style kappo restaurant where interaction with the owner-chef is central to the experience; English-language support is not indicated anywhere, and the menu is written on a board in Japanese with no prices listed.
vegan_detail: This is a traditional kappo restaurant built around seasonal seafood, fish-based dashi (likely katsuobushi/konbu blends), and standard Japanese soy sauce containing wheat. No vegan menu or plant-based options are advertised. Signature dishes include eel, sashimi, beef tendon, and sea urchin. A diner with advance notice might discuss a vegetable-forward arrangement with the chef — he has stated "please speak up casually if there's something you'd like to eat" — but no plant-based or vegan course is documented, and animal-derived dashi is almost certain to appear throughout.
gf_detail: Standard Japanese soy sauce (containing wheat) is a foundational ingredient in kappo cooking at this level. Wheat appears in soy sauce marinades, tare, and likely in many braised and simmered preparations. Tempura is not prominent on documented menus, but shared prep surfaces and no dedicated GF protocols are documented. The chef is known for accommodating individual requests, so a celiac diner should communicate clearly at the time of reservation; however, the kitchen is not certified GF and cross-contamination risk cannot be ruled out.

bio.background: Born in December 1969 in Aichi Prefecture, Fujiwara trained for 16 years at the celebrated Osaka kaiseki restaurant Sakuichi (作一) before returning to Nagoya and opening ふじ原 in 2004. The restaurant relocated and was fully renovated in 2016 to its current third-floor home inside the White Mates building on Hisaya-Odori.
bio.specialty: Seasonal kappo-style Japanese cuisine rooted in Osaka (Naniwa-ryori) tradition: a rotating à la carte board of over 100 items including sashimi assortments, eel prepared two ways, braised beef tendon, and a vibrant five-item appetizer course with sea urchin, fish roe, and spring vegetables.
bio.philosophy: Fujiwara describes his goal as being 'worthy of the name Sakuichi' — a commitment to thorough, quality work inherited from his Osaka training. He emphasises seasonal ingredients and wants guests to feel they can 'speak up casually if there's something they'd like to eat,' making high-end kaiseki feel approachable.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's daily menu is displayed on a board at the entrance rather than printed, with no prices listed — a reflection of the omakase spirit where trust in the chef guides the meal. A 2024 diner reported spending ¥18,480 for roughly two hours including several à la carte dishes and drinks.
ふじ原was selected for the Tabelog Japanese Cuisine 100 Best Restaurants 2025 list, recognising it among Japan's top-tier washoku establishments.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free protocols or kitchen separation is documented. As a traditional kappo kitchen, wheat-containing soy sauce is a near-universal base ingredient across sauces, braises, and marinades, making cross-contamination a realistic concern.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (koikuchi shoyu, which contains wheat) is the expected foundation of this cuisine style. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternatives are mentioned on any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi (likely katsuobushi) is a foundational element of kappo cooking. Seafood and animal proteins are present throughout service. No vegan cross-contact controls are documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The chef has stated guests should 'speak up casually if there's something they'd like to eat,' suggesting some willingness to accommodate requests, but no formal allergy protocol or allergen menu is documented on any platform.

positives.text: Owner-chef format with direct counter interaction means dietary needs can be communicated personally to the person preparing the food, which is more reliable than communicating through front-of-house staff in a large restaurant.

caveats: Seat count varies across sources (Tabelog: 18, Hitosara: 26); Tabelog used as primary. No official standalone website exists beyond the White Mates building page. All dietary safety assessments are inferred from cuisine type — no direct allergy/GF documentation found on any platform. The restaurant has no English-language presence. Hours confirmed from multiple sources but should be verified before visiting as a kappo restaurant may close for private bookings.

neighborhood: 中区二の丸・金シャチ横丁（名古屋城駅徒歩1分）
address_ja: 愛知県名古屋市中区二の丸1-3 金シャチ横丁宗春ゾーン
geocode_note: GSI geocode returned 愛知県名古屋市中区二の丸１番 (chō-level); approximately 273m from Nagoya Castle target point — well within 1.61km radius.
seats_text: 約30席（カウンターあり、カウンターは2組まで）
cultural_comfort_note: Located in Kinshachi Yokocho, a tourist-facing food district built around Nagoya Castle, so staff are accustomed to non-Japanese visitors; basic Japanese greetings will suffice, though menus and staff communication are primarily in Japanese.
gf_detail: Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is used throughout; the menu features tempura (Mikawa clam tempura noted), shari rice seasoned with red and white vinegar (check for mirin), and dashi-based dishes — none of which are labelled gluten-free. No dedicated GF accommodation or tamari option is advertised anywhere. Celiac diners should call ahead and must ask specifically about soy sauce and hidden wheat in dashi and vinegar seasonings.
vegan_detail: Entirely seafood and animal-product focused: nigiri, sashimi, chawanmushi (egg custard), crab cream croquette, eel, Nagoya miso-seasoned beef, and dashi-based preparations. No plant-based alternatives are offered or implied.

bio.background: Muto grew up watching his father run a tonkatsu restaurant called Nabeyaki in Nagoya's Kita Ward. Initially reluctant to follow a culinary path, he was won over after spending one week helping his father and witnessing firsthand how much joy food brought to regular customers. That experience drove him to pursue hospitality with a goal of reaching even more people than his father had.
bio.specialty: Creative Nagoya-style nigiri using both red and white vinegar for the shari, chosen strategically per ingredient; signature shrimp piece arranged to resemble Nagoya's golden kinshachi ornament.
bio.philosophy: Guard the best of traditional Edo-mae sushi while pursuing new challenges others haven't attempted — blending seasonal Nagoya ingredients (Mikawa large clams, Gifu Hatsushimo rice, Nagoya miso beef) with refined craftsmanship.
roles: 寿司職人 (sushi chef at counter)

anecdotes.text: Muto's father told stories of customers' delight every single day with obvious joy. Witnessing that firsthand was the moment Muto committed to the food industry — he wanted to 'expand food's possibilities and reach more people with skills surpassing my father's.'

The head chef at the counter was considered for a headmaster position at a sushi training institution before joining Kinshachi Zushi — underscoring the technical rigour behind the restaurant's approach.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura appears on the menu (Mikawa clam tempura), meaning wheat flour is in active use in the kitchen — cross-contamination risk for celiac diners is real and unaddressed by any public source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned on any platform. Standard wheat-containing Japanese soy sauce must be assumed for all nigiri, sashimi dipping, and seasoning.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is built around seafood and animal products; dashi (fish stock) is almost certainly present in multiple preparations. No vegan cross-contact mitigation is mentioned.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy-handling policy, or staff training information has been disclosed on any public platform. Direct telephone inquiry (052-386-6326) is the only recourse.

positives.text: Omakase counter format means direct chef interaction — a motivated diner can discuss ingredient concerns piece by piece with the person preparing the food.

caveats: Opened October 2025; only 4 Tabelog reviews at time of research. Seat count varies slightly across sources (29–38); 30 used per Tabelog. Hours listed as daily with no regular closed day across all platforms — confirm before visiting as newly opened venues sometimes adjust schedules. No allergen or dietary accommodation data is publicly available; celiac and vegan diners must contact the restaurant directly.

cuisine: 甘味処・抹茶 (Japanese sweets tearoom, matcha)
